# Jairo Portfolio Azure Board Project

## Role

You are an Agile Project Manager consultant with 10+ years of experience in auditing projects using the SAFe framework [https://ScaledAgileFramework.com](https://ScaledAgileFramework.com).

## Goal

Your goal is to audit the project in Azure Board.

## Rules

```gherkin
-Scenario: Creating an audit report
  Given an audit report is created
  Then it should be saved under the `./audit/` sub-folder
  And it should follow the file naming convention of `AUDIT_<date>_<time>.md`

-Scenario: Adhering to framework standards
  Given the project is being audited or mentored
  Then the auditor must always follow SAFe framework standards and best practices

-Scenario: Visualizing data
  Given data is being presented in the report
Then the auditor must always create diagrams to visualize the data

-Scenario: Generating PDF reports
  Given an audit report is being generated as a PDF
  Then it must be saved in the same folder as the audit report
```

1→# SAFe Audit Report — Jairosoft Portfolio 2→ 3→**Audit Date:** 2026-02-25 4→**Auditor:** Claude AI (Agile PM Consultant) 5→**Framework:** Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) 6.0 6→**Organization:** Jairosoft LLC (`dev.azure.com/jairo`) 7→**Project:** Jairosoft Portfolio 8→ 9→--- 10→ 11→## 1. Executive Summary 12→ 13→The Jairosoft Portfolio project is a multi-team, multi-product initiative managed through Azure DevOps with SAFe-aligned practices. The project spans 21 teams organized across Agile Release Trains (ARTs), with work structured through Program Increments (PIs). The portfolio currently operates across two primary product domains — **Colina Health** and **Sitecore** — alongside training, marketing, and operational functions delivered through the **Jairo Institute of Technology (JIT)**. 14→ 15→### Overall SAFe Maturity Assessment 16→ 17→| Dimension | Score | Rating | 18→|---|---|---| 19→| Team Organization | 7/10 | Good | 20→| PI Cadence & Iterations | 8/10 | Strong | 21→| Backlog Hierarchy | 8/10 | Strong | 22→| Work Item Discipline | 5/10 | Needs Improvement | 23→| CI/CD Pipeline Health | 6/10 | Moderate | 24→| Lean Portfolio Management | 7/10 | Good | 25→ 26→**Overall Score: 6.8 / 10 — Moderate SAFe Maturity** 27→ 28→--- 29→ 30→## 2. Organizational Structure 31→ 32→The project has **21 teams**, which maps well to a SAFe Agile Release Train (ART) structure. 33→ 34→### Team Categories 35→ 36→```mermaid 37→graph LR 38→ subgraph "Portfolio Level" 39→ LPM["Jairosoft Lean Portfolio Management"] 40→ PM["Program Management Team"] 41→ end 42→ subgraph "Agile Teams (Development)" 43→ B["Bravo Agile Team"] 44→ W["Whiskey Agile Team"] 45→ U["Unicorn Agile Team"] 46→ C["Charlie Agile Team"] 47→ end 48→ subgraph "Product Teams" 49→ JIT["JIT Site eLMS Product Team"] 50→ CH["Colina Health Product Team"] 51→ MS["Marketing Site Product Team"] 52→ end 53→ subgraph "Shared Services" 54→ UX["UI UX Design Team"] 55→ DO["DevOps IT System Team"] 56→ AI["AI Coalition Team"] 57→ FO["FinOps SuccessForPeople Team"] 58→ MK["Marketing Sales Team"] 59→ TP["Technical Pre-Sales Team"] 60→ JO["JIT Operation Team"] 61→ JP["Jairosoft Project Team"] 62→ end 63→ subgraph "Intern Teams" 64→ IW["Interns Whiskey Team"] 65→ ID["Interns DevOps IT Team"] 66→ IU["Interns Unicorn Team"] 67→ IDe["Interns Design Team"] 68→ end 69→ LPM --> PM 70→ PM --> B & W & U & C 71→ PM --> JIT & CH & MS 72→``` 73→ 74→### Findings — Team Organization 75→ 76→**Strengths:** 77→ 78→- Clear SAFe hierarchy with a **Lean Portfolio Management** team at the top, which is a SAFe best practice for portfolio-level governance. 79→- **Program Management Team** exists to coordinate across ARTs. 80→- Named Agile teams (Bravo, Whiskey, Unicorn, Charlie) suggest structured team identity — a hallmark of healthy SAFe adoption. 81→- Dedicated **Product Teams** for each value stream (JIT eLMS, Colina Health, Marketing Site). 82→- **Shared Services** teams (DevOps IT, AI Coalition, UI/UX Design) properly support cross-cutting concerns. 83→ 84→**Concerns:** 85→ 86→- **21 teams is high** for a single ART. SAFe recommends 5–12 teams per ART. Consider whether this portfolio should be restructured into **2–3 ARTs** (e.g., Colina Health ART, JIT/Education ART, Marketing/Sales ART). 87→- **4 Intern teams** may create coordination overhead. Consider embedding interns within established Agile teams rather than maintaining separate teams. 88→- Several teams lack descriptions, making it hard to understand their scope and charter. 89→ 90→--- 91→ 92→## 3. PI Cadence & Iteration Structure 93→ 94→The portfolio follows a well-defined PI cadence with the following timeline: 95→ 96→### PI Timeline 97→ 98→| PI | Start Date | End Date | Duration | Status | 99→|---|---|---|---|---| 100→| PI 0 | Sep 16, 2024 | Dec 29, 2024 | ~15 weeks | Completed | 101→| PI 1 | Dec 30, 2024 | Mar 23, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | 102→| PI 2 | Mar 24, 2025 | Jun 15, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | 103→| PI 3 | Jun 16, 2025 | Sep 07, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | 104→| PI 4 | Sep 08, 2025 | Nov 30, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | 105→| PI 5 | Dec 01, 2025 | Feb 22, 2026 | ~12 weeks | **Just Completed** | 106→ 107→### PI 3 Iteration Breakdown (Detail Available) 108→ 109→```mermaid 110→gantt 111→ title PI 3 Iteration Schedule 112→ dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD 113→ section PI 3 114→ Iteration 3.1 :2025-06-16, 2025-06-29 115→ Iteration 3.2 :2025-06-30, 2025-07-13 116→ Iteration 3.3 :2025-07-14, 2025-07-27 117→ Iteration 3.4 :2025-07-28, 2025-08-10 118→ Iteration 3.5 :2025-08-11, 2025-08-24 119→ Iteration 3.6 (IP) :2025-08-25, 2025-09-07 120→``` 121→ 122→### Findings — PI Cadence 123→ 124→**Strengths:** 125→ 126→- Consistent **12-week PI cadence** from PI 1 onward (PI 0 was a 15-week onboarding/setup PI, which is acceptable). 127→- PI 3 shows proper **2-week iteration cadence** with 5 development iterations + 1 Innovation & Planning (IP) iteration — this is textbook SAFe. 128→- The IP iteration (3.6) is correctly labeled and positioned at the end of the PI. 129→- **6 PIs completed** in ~17 months demonstrates commitment and velocity. 130→ 131→**Concerns:** 132→ 133→- As of Feb 25, 2026, PI 5 just ended (Feb 22). No PI 6 iteration structure is visible — this suggests **PI Planning may be delayed** or hasn't been configured yet in ADO. 134→- PI 0 was 3 weeks longer than subsequent PIs. While acceptable for a launch PI, the variance should be documented. 135→- Only PI 3 has visible sub-iterations; PIs 0, 1, 2, 4, and 5 do not show iteration breakdowns at the portfolio level. 136→ 137→**Recommendation:** Ensure PI 6 planning is scheduled immediately. The 3-day gap between PIs should be used for Inspect & Adapt (I&A) and PI Planning events. 138→ 139→--- 140→ 141→## 4. Backlog Hierarchy & Work Item Types 142→ 143→The portfolio uses a customized SAFe backlog hierarchy: 144→ 145→```mermaid 146→graph TD 147→ L5["Goals & Releases
(Goal, Release)"] 148→ L4["Epics & Objectives
(Epic, PI Objective)"] 149→ L3["Features & Enablers
(Feature, Enabler Feature, Change Request)"] 150→ L2["Stories & Deliverables
(User Story, Enabler, Spike, Defect,
Courseware, Training, Design, Tech Debt)"] 151→ L1["Tasks
(Task, Peer Review)"] 152→ L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1 153→ style L5 fill:#60af49,color:#fff 154→ style L4 fill:#E06C00,color:#fff 155→ style L3 fill:#773B93,color:#fff 156→ style L2 fill:#0098C7,color:#fff 157→ style L1 fill:#A4880A,color:#fff 158→``` 159→ 160→### Findings — Backlog Hierarchy 161→ 162→**Strengths:** 163→ 164→- **5-level hierarchy** aligns well with SAFe's portfolio, large solution, program, and team levels. 165→- Custom work item types like **Courseware**, **Training**, and **Design** show thoughtful adaptation of SAFe to the organization's domain (education/training). 166→- **PI Objective** as a work item type at the Epic level is a strong SAFe practice. 167→- **Risks and Impediments** backlog (Issue, Risk) is defined — this supports ROAM risk management. 168→- **Peer Review** as a task type promotes quality practices. 169→ 170→**Concerns:** 171→ 172→- The **Features and Enablers** and **Stories and Deliverables** backlogs are marked as **hidden** (`isHidden: true`). This could mean teams aren't using the board views at these levels, losing visibility. 173→- The **Risks and Impediments** backlog is also hidden — risk management visibility may be lacking. 174→- **8 work item types** at the Story level is high. Consider whether Spike, Courseware, Training, and Design could be consolidated or tracked via tags instead. 175→ 176→--- 177→ 178→## 5. Work Item Health Analysis 179→ 180→Based on the 50 sampled work items: 181→ 182→### State Distribution 183→ 184→| State | Count | % | 185→|---|---|---| 186→| Closed | 30 | 60% | 187→| New | 8 | 16% | 188→| Blocked | 3 | 6% | 189→| Removed | 3 | 6% | 190→| Requirements Gathering | 4 | 8% | 191→| Grooming | 1 | 2% | 192→| Other | 1 | 2% | 193→ 194→### Work Item Type Distribution 195→ 196→```mermaid 197→pie title Work Item Types (Sampled) 198→ "Spike" : 21 199→ "Enabler" : 8 200→ "Training" : 4 201→ "Courseware" : 5 202→ "User Story" : 3 203→ "Task" : 7 204→ "Other" : 2 205→``` 206→ 207→### Key Team Members 208→ 209→| Team Member | Work Items | Primary Types | 210→|---|---|---| 211→| armelita@jairosoft.com | 13 | Training, Enabler, Courseware | 212→| Jeffrey Avila | 7 | Spike (Marketing) | 213→| Roche Casipong | 7 | Spike (Registration/Sales) | 214→| grace@jairosoft.com | 5 | User Story, Courseware | 215→| Elmer Joseph Cutin | 3 | Spike (Marketing) | 216→| Vicsante Aseniero | 3 | Spike, Enabler | 217→| Ramon Aseniero | 2 | Spike, User Story | 218→ 219→### Findings — Work Items 220→ 221→**Strengths:** 222→ 223→- **60% closure rate** on sampled items shows work is getting done. 224→- Clear ownership — most work items have assigned team members. 225→- Work spans multiple domains: marketing, training/courseware development, product development (Colina Health), and operations. 226→ 227→**Critical Concerns:** 228→ 229→- **Spikes dominate at 42%** of all work items. SAFe recommends spikes as time-boxed research activities, not the primary work type. This suggests the organization may be over-using Spikes instead of proper User Stories with acceptance criteria. 230→- **User Stories are only 6%** of sampled work — this is alarmingly low. Value delivery should be primarily tracked through User Stories. 231→- **3 Blocked items (6%)** with no visible resolution path. Blocked items tagged "OnHold" suggest systemic impediments that aren't being actively resolved. 232→- **3 Removed items (6%)** indicate scope instability or poor backlog refinement. 233→- **Items in "Requirements Gathering" and "Grooming" states** suggest some work items are languishing in pre-development stages. 234→- **No Story Points visible** on any sampled work items — velocity tracking may not be active. 235→ 236→--- 237→ 238→## 6. Sitecore Environment Creation | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | 251→| Sitecore Environment Deletion | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | **Partial Failure** | 252→| Sitecore-CD Repository Creation | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | 253→| Sitecore-CM Repository Creation | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | 254→| Sitecore Repository Deletion | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | 255→| Sitecore | Sitecore | Jul 9, 2025 | Success | 256→| SugConPH | Sitecore | Aug 15, 2025 | Success | 257→ 258→### Pipeline Activity Timeline 259→ 260→```mermaid 261→gantt 262→ title Pipeline Last Build Activity 263→ dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD 264→ section Active (2026) 265→ BE Backend CICD :done, 2026-02-09, 1d 266→ FE Frontend CICD :done, 2026-02-05, 1d 267→ section Stale (2025) 268→ Colina BE Docker :crit, 2025-03-10, 1d 269→ Colina FE Docker :crit, 2025-03-11, 1d 270→ ColinaHealth-API Go :crit, 2025-03-06, 1d 271→ colinahealth.git :crit, 2025-02-05, 1d 272→ Sitecore Env Create :crit, 2025-08-14, 1d 273→ Sitecore Env Delete :crit, 2025-08-14, 1d 274→ Sitecore :crit, 2025-07-09, 1d 275→ SugConPH :crit, 2025-08-15, 1d 276→``` 277→ 278→### Findings — CI/CD 279→ 280→**Strengths:** 281→ 282→- **13 pipelines** across two product domains shows CI/CD investment. 283→- The two most recent builds (Feb 2026) are for Colina Health, indicating active development on that product. 284→- All builds except one show successful status. 285→ 286→**Concerns:** 287→ 288→- **11 of 13 pipelines haven't been triggered since 2025** — this means 85% of pipelines are stale. Either the codebases are dormant or builds aren't being triggered regularly. 289→- **Sitecore pipelines** last ran in Aug 2025 (6+ months ago) — if Sitecore is still an active product, this is a red flag for continuous integration practices. 290→- The **Sitecore Environment Deletion** pipeline shows a partial failure that was never addressed. 291→- Only **Colina Health Backend and Frontend** pipelines show recent (2026) activity, suggesting development effort is concentrated on a single product. 292→ 293→--- 294→ 295→## 7. Repository Analysis 296→ 297→### Repository Inventory (9 Repos) 298→ 299→| Repository | Size | Domain | 300→|---|---|---| 301→| Sitecore-CD | 236 MB | Sitecore | 302→| Sitecore-CM | 187 MB | Sitecore | 303→| BEColinaHealth-GO | 42 MB | Colina Health | 304→| SugConPH | 31 MB | Sitecore | 305→| colinahealth.git | 30 MB | Colina Health | 306→| FEColinaHealth-GO | 19 MB | Colina Health | 307→| BEColinaHealth.git | 3.5 MB | Colina Health | 308→| Sitecore | 2.6 MB | Sitecore | 309→| JairoDevOps | <1 KB | DevOps | 310→ 311→### Findings — Repositories 312→ 313→- **Sitecore repos are the largest** (423 MB combined) but show no recent pipeline activity — potential tech debt accumulation. 314→- **Colina Health** has both legacy (.git suffix) and newer (GO suffix) repos, suggesting a technology migration from one stack to Go. 315→- **JairoDevOps** repo is essentially empty (723 bytes) — should be cleaned up or documented. 316→ 317→--- 318→ 319→## 8. SAFe Compliance Assessment 320→ 321→### SAFe Core Values Alignment 322→ 323→| SAFe Core Value | Status | Evidence | 324→|---|---|---| 325→| **Alignment** | Partial | PI cadence exists; PI Objectives are defined as work item types. However, no visible Strategic Themes or portfolio Kanban flow. | 326→| **Built-in Quality** | Needs Improvement | Peer Review task type exists, but no evidence of Definition of Done, automated testing metrics, or code review policies. | 327→| **Transparency** | Partial | Work items are tracked, but key backlogs (Features, Stories, Risks) are hidden from board views. | 328→| **Program Execution** | Good | 6 PIs completed with consistent cadence. Teams are structured and work is assigned. | 329→ 330→### SAFe Essential Practices Checklist 331→ 332→| Practice | Implemented? | Notes | 333→|---|---|---| 334→| PI Planning | Likely | PI structure exists, but no formal PI Planning artifacts visible | 335→| Iteration Planning | Partial | Only PI 3 shows sub-iterations | 336→| Iteration Review/Demo | Unknown | No evidence in ADO | 337→| Iteration Retrospective | Unknown | No evidence in ADO | 338→| PI System Demo | Unknown | No evidence in ADO | 339→| Inspect & Adapt | Unknown | No formal I&A artifacts visible | 340→| Backlog Refinement | Partial | Items in "Grooming" state suggest some refinement | 341→| Continuous Integration | Partial | Pipelines exist but most are stale | 342→| Continuous Deployment | Partial | Docker-based pipelines suggest CD capability for Colina Health | 343→| PI Objectives | Yes | PI Objective work item type defined at Epic level | 344→| WSJF Prioritization | Unknown | No business value or time criticality data visible on items | 345→ 346→--- 347→ 348→## 9. Recommendations 349→ 350→### Immediate Actions (This PI) 351→ 352→1. **Plan PI 6 immediately.** PI 5 ended Feb 22 — schedule PI Planning within the next week to avoid losing momentum. 353→2. **Address 3 Blocked work items.** Conduct an impediment removal session; items tagged "OnHold" need executive escalation or formal removal. 354→3. **Unhide Feature and Story backlogs.** Teams need board-level visibility at these levels for proper flow management. 355→ 356→### Short-Term (Next 1–2 PIs) 357→ 358→4. **Reduce Spike usage.** Convert ongoing research activities into proper User Stories with acceptance criteria. Target <15% Spikes in the backlog. 359→5. **Implement Story Points.** Without velocity data, predictability and capacity planning are impossible. 360→6. **Reactivate or archive stale pipelines.** 11 dormant pipelines create confusion; either trigger regular builds or formally decommission them. 361→7. **Restructure into 2–3 ARTs.** With 21 teams, consider splitting into Colina Health ART, JIT/Education ART, and Marketing/Operations ART. 362→ 363→### Long-Term (Next 2–4 PIs) 364→ 365→8. **Implement WSJF prioritization.** Add Business Value and Time Criticality fields to Features and Enablers. 366→9. **Embed interns into existing teams.** Reduce the 4 separate intern teams to improve mentoring and reduce coordination overhead. 367→10. **Establish Inspect & Adapt cadence.** Formalize I&A ceremonies at the end of each PI with documented improvement backlog. 368→11. **Clean up repository landscape.** Archive or document the empty JairoDevOps repo; assess whether legacy Colina Health repos (non-GO) should be deprecated. 369→ 370→--- 371→ 372→## 10. # SAFe Audit Report — Jairosoft Portfolio **Audit Date:** 2026-02-25 **Auditor:** Claude AI (Agile PM Consultant) **Framework:** Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) 6.0 **Organization:** Jairosoft LLC (`dev.azure.com/jairo`) **Project:** Jairosoft Portfolio --- ## 1. Executive Summary The Jairosoft Portfolio project is a multi-team, multi-product initiative managed through Azure DevOps with SAFe-aligned practices. The project spans 21 teams organized across Agile Release Trains (ARTs), with work structured through Program Increments (PIs). The portfolio currently operates across two primary product domains — **Colina Health** and **Sitecore** — alongside training, marketing, and operational functions delivered through the **Jairo Institute of Technology (JIT)**. ### Overall SAFe Maturity Assessment | Dimension | Score | Rating | |---|---|---| | Team Organization | 7/10 | Good | | PI Cadence & Iterations | 8/10 | Strong | | Backlog Hierarchy | 8/10 | Strong | | Work Item Discipline | 5/10 | Needs Improvement | | CI/CD Pipeline Health | 6/10 | Moderate | | Lean Portfolio Management | 7/10 | Good | **Overall Score: 6.8 / 10 — Moderate SAFe Maturity** --- ## 2. Organizational Structure The project has **21 teams**, which maps well to a SAFe Agile Release Train (ART) structure. ### Team Categories ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Portfolio Level" LPM["Jairosoft Lean Portfolio Management"] PM["Program Management Team"] end subgraph "Agile Teams (Development)" B["Bravo Agile Team"] W["Whiskey Agile Team"] U["Unicorn Agile Team"] C["Charlie Agile Team"] end subgraph "Product Teams" JIT["JIT Site eLMS Product Team"] CH["Colina Health Product Team"] MS["Marketing Site Product Team"] end subgraph "Shared Services" UX["UI UX Design Team"] DO["DevOps IT System Team"] AI["AI Coalition Team"] FO["FinOps SuccessForPeople Team"] MK["Marketing Sales Team"] TP["Technical Pre-Sales Team"] JO["JIT Operation Team"] JP["Jairosoft Project Team"] end subgraph "Intern Teams" IW["Interns Whiskey Team"] ID["Interns DevOps IT Team"] IU["Interns Unicorn Team"] IDe["Interns Design Team"] end LPM --> PM PM --> B & W & U & C PM --> JIT & CH & MS ``` ### Findings — Team Organization **Strengths:** - Clear SAFe hierarchy with a **Lean Portfolio Management** team at the top, which is a SAFe best practice for portfolio-level governance. - **Program Management Team** exists to coordinate across ARTs. - Named Agile teams (Bravo, Whiskey, Unicorn, Charlie) suggest structured team identity — a hallmark of healthy SAFe adoption. - Dedicated **Product Teams** for each value stream (JIT eLMS, Colina Health, Marketing Site). - **Shared Services** teams (DevOps IT, AI Coalition, UI/UX Design) properly support cross-cutting concerns. **Concerns:** - **21 teams is high** for a single ART. SAFe recommends 5–12 teams per ART. Consider whether this portfolio should be restructured into **2–3 ARTs** (e.g., Colina Health ART, JIT/Education ART, Marketing/Sales ART). - **4 Intern teams** may create coordination overhead. Consider embedding interns within established Agile teams rather than maintaining separate teams. - Several teams lack descriptions, making it hard to understand their scope and charter. --- ## 3. PI Cadence & Iteration Structure The portfolio follows a well-defined PI cadence with the following timeline: ### PI Timeline | PI | Start Date | End Date | Duration | Status | |---|---|---|---|---| | PI 0 | Sep 16, 2024 | Dec 29, 2024 | ~15 weeks | Completed | | PI 1 | Dec 30, 2024 | Mar 23, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 2 | Mar 24, 2025 | Jun 15, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 3 | Jun 16, 2025 | Sep 07, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 4 | Sep 08, 2025 | Nov 30, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 5 | Dec 01, 2025 | Feb 22, 2026 | ~12 weeks | **Just Completed** | ### PI 3 Iteration Breakdown (Detail Available) ```mermaid gantt title PI 3 Iteration Schedule dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD section PI 3 Iteration 3.1 :2025-06-16, 2025-06-29 Iteration 3.2 :2025-06-30, 2025-07-13 Iteration 3.3 :2025-07-14, 2025-07-27 Iteration 3.4 :2025-07-28, 2025-08-10 Iteration 3.5 :2025-08-11, 2025-08-24 Iteration 3.6 (IP) :2025-08-25, 2025-09-07 ``` ### Findings — PI Cadence **Strengths:** - Consistent **12-week PI cadence** from PI 1 onward (PI 0 was a 15-week onboarding/setup PI, which is acceptable). - PI 3 shows proper **2-week iteration cadence** with 5 development iterations + 1 Innovation & Planning (IP) iteration — this is textbook SAFe. - The IP iteration (3.6) is correctly labeled and positioned at the end of the PI. - **6 PIs completed** in ~17 months demonstrates commitment and velocity. **Concerns:** - As of Feb 25, 2026, PI 5 just ended (Feb 22). No PI 6 iteration structure is visible — this suggests **PI Planning may be delayed** or hasn't been configured yet in ADO. - PI 0 was 3 weeks longer than subsequent PIs. While acceptable for a launch PI, the variance should be documented. - Only PI 3 has visible sub-iterations; PIs 0, 1, 2, 4, and 5 do not show iteration breakdowns at the portfolio level. **Recommendation:** Ensure PI 6 planning is scheduled immediately. The 3-day gap between PIs should be used for Inspect & Adapt (I&A) and PI Planning events. --- ## 4. Backlog Hierarchy & Work Item Types The portfolio uses a customized SAFe backlog hierarchy: ```mermaid graph TD L5["Goals & Releases
(Goal, Release)"] L4["Epics & Objectives
(Epic, PI Objective)"] L3["Features & Enablers
(Feature, Enabler Feature, Change Request)"] L2["Stories & Deliverables
(User Story, Enabler, Spike, Defect,
Courseware, Training, Design, Tech Debt)"] L1["Tasks
(Task, Peer Review)"] L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1 style L5 fill:#60af49,color:#fff style L4 fill:#E06C00,color:#fff style L3 fill:#773B93,color:#fff style L2 fill:#0098C7,color:#fff style L1 fill:#A4880A,color:#fff ``` ### Findings — Backlog Hierarchy **Strengths:** - **5-level hierarchy** aligns well with SAFe's portfolio, large solution, program, and team levels. - Custom work item types like **Courseware**, **Training**, and **Design** show thoughtful adaptation of SAFe to the organization's domain (education/training). - **PI Objective** as a work item type at the Epic level is a strong SAFe practice. - **Risks and Impediments** backlog (Issue, Risk) is defined — this supports ROAM risk management. - **Peer Review** as a task type promotes quality practices. **Concerns:** - The **Features and Enablers** and **Stories and Deliverables** backlogs are marked as **hidden** (`isHidden: true`). This could mean teams aren't using the board views at these levels, losing visibility. - The **Risks and Impediments** backlog is also hidden — risk management visibility may be lacking. - **8 work item types** at the Story level is high. Consider whether Spike, Courseware, Training, and Design could be consolidated or tracked via tags instead. --- ## 5. Work Item Health Analysis Based on the 50 sampled work items: ### State Distribution | State | Count | % | |---|---|---| | Closed | 30 | 60% | | New | 8 | 16% | | Blocked | 3 | 6% | | Removed | 3 | 6% | | Requirements Gathering | 4 | 8% | | Grooming | 1 | 2% | | Other | 1 | 2% | ### Work Item Type Distribution ```mermaid pie title Work Item Types (Sampled) "Spike" : 21 "Enabler" : 8 "Training" : 4 "Courseware" : 5 "User Story" : 3 "Task" : 7 "Other" : 2 ``` ### Key Team Members | Team Member | Work Items | Primary Types | |---|---|---| | armelita@jairosoft.com | 13 | Training, Enabler, Courseware | | Jeffrey Avila | 7 | Spike (Marketing) | | Roche Casipong | 7 | Spike (Registration/Sales) | | grace@jairosoft.com | 5 | User Story, Courseware | | Elmer Joseph Cutin | 3 | Spike (Marketing) | | Vicsante Aseniero | 3 | Spike, Enabler | | Ramon Aseniero | 2 | Spike, User Story | ### Findings — Work Items **Strengths:** - **60% closure rate** on sampled items shows work is getting done. - Clear ownership — most work items have assigned team members. - Work spans multiple domains: marketing, training/courseware development, product development (Colina Health), and operations. **Critical Concerns:** - **Spikes dominate at 42%** of all work items. SAFe recommends spikes as time-boxed research activities, not the primary work type. This suggests the organization may be over-using Spikes instead of proper User Stories with acceptance criteria. - **User Stories are only 6%** of sampled work — this is alarmingly low. Value delivery should be primarily tracked through User Stories. - **3 Blocked items (6%)** with no visible resolution path. Blocked items tagged "OnHold" suggest systemic impediments that aren't being actively resolved. - **3 Removed items (6%)** indicate scope instability or poor backlog refinement. - **Items in "Requirements Gathering" and "Grooming" states** suggest some work items are languishing in pre-development stages. - **No Story Points visible** on any sampled work items — velocity tracking may not be active. --- ## 6. CI/CD Pipeline Health ### Pipeline Inventory (13 Pipelines) | Pipeline | Domain | Last Build | Status | |---|---|---|---| | BEColinaHealth.git Backend CICD | Colina Health | Feb 9, 2026 | Success | | colinahealth.git FrontEnd CICD | Colina Health | Feb 5, 2026 | Success | | Colina BE Docker Image | Colina Health | Mar 10, 2025 | Success | | Colina FE Docker Image | Colina Health | Mar 11, 2025 | Success | | ColinaHealth-API_Golang_Build | Colina Health | Mar 6, 2025 | Success | | colinahealth.git | Colina Health | Feb 5, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore Environment Creation | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore Environment Deletion | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | **Partial Failure** | | Sitecore-CD Repository Creation | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore-CM Repository Creation | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore Repository Deletion | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore | Sitecore | Jul 9, 2025 | Success | | SugConPH | Sitecore | Aug 15, 2025 | Success | ### Pipeline Activity Timeline ```mermaid gantt title Pipeline Last Build Activity dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD section Active (2026) BE Backend CICD :done, 2026-02-09, 1d FE Frontend CICD :done, 2026-02-05, 1d section Stale (2025) Colina BE Docker :crit, 2025-03-10, 1d Colina FE Docker :crit, 2025-03-11, 1d ColinaHealth-API Go :crit, 2025-03-06, 1d colinahealth.git :crit, 2025-02-05, 1d Sitecore Env Create :crit, 2025-08-14, 1d Sitecore Env Delete :crit, 2025-08-14, 1d Sitecore :crit, 2025-07-09, 1d SugConPH :crit, 2025-08-15, 1d ``` ### Findings — CI/CD **Strengths:** - **13 pipelines** across two product domains shows CI/CD investment. - The two most recent builds (Feb 2026) are for Colina Health, indicating active development on that product. - All builds except one show successful status. **Concerns:** - **11 of 13 pipelines haven't been triggered since 2025** — this means 85% of pipelines are stale. Either the codebases are dormant or builds aren't being triggered regularly. - **Sitecore pipelines** last ran in Aug 2025 (6+ months ago) — if Sitecore is still an active product, this is a red flag for continuous integration practices. - The **Sitecore Environment Deletion** pipeline shows a partial failure that was never addressed. - Only **Colina Health Backend and Frontend** pipelines show recent (2026) activity, suggesting development effort is concentrated on a single product. --- ## 7. Repository Analysis ### Repository Inventory (9 Repos) | Repository | Size | Domain | |---|---|---| | Sitecore-CD | 236 MB | Sitecore | | Sitecore-CM | 187 MB | Sitecore | | BEColinaHealth-GO | 42 MB | Colina Health | | SugConPH | 31 MB | Sitecore | | colinahealth.git | 30 MB | Colina Health | | FEColinaHealth-GO | 19 MB | Colina Health | | BEColinaHealth.git | 3.5 MB | Colina Health | | Sitecore | 2.6 MB | Sitecore | | JairoDevOps | <1 KB | DevOps | ### Findings — Repositories - **Sitecore repos are the largest** (423 MB combined) but show no recent pipeline activity — potential tech debt accumulation. - **Colina Health** has both legacy (.git suffix) and newer (GO suffix) repos, suggesting a technology migration from one stack to Go. - **JairoDevOps** repo is essentially empty (723 bytes) — should be cleaned up or documented. --- ## 8. SAFe Compliance Assessment ### SAFe Core Values Alignment | SAFe Core Value | Status | Evidence | |---|---|---| | **Alignment** | Partial | PI cadence exists; PI Objectives are defined as work item types. However, no visible Strategic Themes or portfolio Kanban flow. | | **Built-in Quality** | Needs Improvement | Peer Review task type exists, but no evidence of Definition of Done, automated testing metrics, or code review policies. | | **Transparency** | Partial | Work items are tracked, but key backlogs (Features, Stories, Risks) are hidden from board views. | | **Program Execution** | Good | 6 PIs completed with consistent cadence. Teams are structured and work is assigned. | ### SAFe Essential Practices Checklist | Practice | Implemented? | Notes | |---|---|---| | PI Planning | Likely | PI structure exists, but no formal PI Planning artifacts visible | | Iteration Planning | Partial | Only PI 3 shows sub-iterations | | Iteration Review/Demo | Unknown | No evidence in ADO | | Iteration Retrospective | Unknown | No evidence in ADO | | PI System Demo | Unknown | No evidence in ADO | | Inspect & Adapt | Unknown | No formal I&A artifacts visible | | Backlog Refinement | Partial | Items in "Grooming" state suggest some refinement | | Continuous Integration | Partial | Pipelines exist but most are stale | | Continuous Deployment | Partial | Docker-based pipelines suggest CD capability for Colina Health | | PI Objectives | Yes | PI Objective work item type defined at Epic level | | WSJF Prioritization | Unknown | No business value or time criticality data visible on items | --- ## 9. Recommendations ### Immediate Actions (This PI) 1. **Plan PI 6 immediately.** PI 5 ended Feb 22 — schedule PI Planning within the next week to avoid losing momentum. 2. **Address 3 Blocked work items.** Conduct an impediment removal session; items tagged "OnHold" need executive escalation or formal removal. 3. **Unhide Feature and Story backlogs.** Teams need board-level visibility at these levels for proper flow management. ### Short-Term (Next 1–2 PIs) 4. **Reduce Spike usage.** Convert ongoing research activities into proper User Stories with acceptance criteria. Target <15% Spikes in the backlog. 5. **Implement Story Points.** Without velocity data, predictability and capacity planning are impossible. 6. **Reactivate or archive stale pipelines.** 11 dormant pipelines create confusion; either trigger regular builds or formally decommission them. 7. **Restructure into 2–3 ARTs.** With 21 teams, consider splitting into Colina Health ART, JIT/Education ART, and Marketing/Operations ART. ### Long-Term (Next 2–4 PIs) 8. **Implement WSJF prioritization.** Add Business Value and Time Criticality fields to Features and Enablers. 9. **Embed interns into existing teams.** Reduce the 4 separate intern teams to improve mentoring and reduce coordination overhead. 10. **Establish Inspect & Adapt cadence.** Formalize I&A ceremonies at the end of each PI with documented improvement backlog. 11. **Clean up repository landscape.** Archive or document the empty JairoDevOps repo; assess whether legacy Colina Health repos (non-GO) should be deprecated. --- ## 10. Summary Scorecard ```mermaid quadrantChart title SAFe Maturity - Jairosoft Portfolio x-axis Low Maturity --> High Maturity y-axis Low Impact --> High Impact quadrant-1 Invest Now quadrant-2 Strong Foundation quadrant-3 Low Priority quadrant-4 Quick Wins "PI Cadence": [0.75, 0.85] "Team Structure": [0.65, 0.80] "Backlog Hierarchy": [0.70, 0.70] "Work Item Discipline": [0.35, 0.90] "CI/CD Health": [0.45, 0.75] "Velocity Tracking": [0.15, 0.85] "Risk Management": [0.30, 0.60] "LPM Governance": [0.60, 0.65] ``` --- *Report generated by Claude AI Agile PM Consultant on 2026-02-25. Based on SAFe 6.0 framework standards from [ScaledAgileFramework.com](https://ScaledAgileFramework.com).*

content.text:
Here's what I've found after studying the project:

---

## Project Overview: `ado_jit` — Azure DevOps SAFe Audit Workspace

This is a **SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework) audit project** for the **Jairosoft Portfolio** managed in Azure DevOps (`dev.azure.com/jairo`). It is not a code repository — it's an **auditing workspace** that connects to Azure DevOps via MCP tools to inspect and assess agile practices.

### Structure

```
ado_jit/
├── CLAUDE.md          # Project instructions (role, rules, conventions)
├── audit/             # Audit report output folder
│   └── AUDIT_2026-02-25_0030.md   # First completed audit report
├── logs/              # Hook logs (status line, pre-tool-use, prompt submit)
└── .claude/           # Session data
```

### How It Works

1. **CLAUDE.md** defines the AI's role as an Agile PM consultant using SAFe 6.0. It sets rules in Gherkin syntax:
   - Reports go to `./audit/` with naming convention `AUDIT_<date>_<time>.md`
   - Must follow SAFe standards
   - Must include diagrams to visualize data
   - PDF reports must be co-located with the markdown

2. **Azure DevOps MCP integration** — the project has access to `mcp__azure-devops__*` tools for querying Azure Boards, repos, pipelines, work items, iterations, teams, and more from the `Jairosoft Portfolio` project.

3. **Existing audit report** (`AUDIT_2026-02-25_0030.md`) — a comprehensive 396-line SAFe audit that already covers:
   - **21 teams** across the organization (recommends splitting into 2-3 ARTs)
   - **6 completed PIs** (PI 0 through PI 5) with 12-week cadence
   - **5-level backlog hierarchy** with custom work item types (Courseware, Training, Design)
   - **Work item health** — 42% Spikes (too high), only 6% User Stories (too low)
   - **13 CI/CD pipelines** — 85% are stale (last run in 2025)
   - **9 repositories** across Colina Health and Sitecore domains
   - **Overall SAFe maturity score: 6.8/10** (Moderate)
   - Includes Mermaid diagrams for team structure, Gantt charts, pie charts, and quadrant charts

### Key Findings from the Existing Audit

| Area | Score | Top Issue |
|---|---|---|
| Team Organization | 7/10 | 21 teams exceeds SAFe's 5-12 per ART recommendation |
| PI Cadence | 8/10 | PI 6 not yet planned (PI 5 ended Feb 22) |
| Backlog Hierarchy | 8/10 | Feature/Story backlogs hidden from board views |
| Work Item Discipline | 5/10 | Spikes overused, User Stories underused, no Story Points |
| CI/CD Pipeline Health | 6/10 | 85% of pipelines stale since 2025 |
| Lean Portfolio Mgmt | 7/10 | No WSJF prioritization or Strategic Themes visible |

### What You Can Do Next

Since this project is integrated with Azure DevOps MCP tools, I can:
- **Run a fresh audit** pulling live data from Azure Boards
- **Deep-dive into specific areas** (e.g., a specific team's backlog, pipeline details, work item aging)
- **Generate a follow-up audit** comparing current state to the previous report's recommendations
- **Query specific work items, iterations, or repos** for detailed analysis

What would you like to explore?

content.text:
I now have the full picture. Here's the comprehensive analysis of the JIT Operation Team board.

---

## JIT Operation Team — Board Analysis

### At a Glance

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| **Total Work Items** | **84** (6 Epics + 36 Features + 42 Stories) |
| **Current Iteration** | Iteration 6.4 (Feb 23 – Mar 8, 2026) |
| **Current PI** | 2026-PI6 |
| **Risks/Impediments Logged** | **0** |
| **Total Story Points (Stories level)** | **32 pts** across 24 of 42 items |

---

### 1. Epics (PI Objectives) — 6 Items

All 6 are **PI Objectives** (correct SAFe practice). They define PI 6 goals:

| ID | Title | State |
|---|---|---|
| 197441 | Produce Professional Alumni | Active |
| 197443 | Recognized as CSS NC II Assessment Center | Active |
| 197445 | Launch new courses | Active |
| 197444 | CSS NC II Assessment Offering | New |
| 197446 | Use OpenEduCat for JIT Operations | New |
| 197450 | Onboard Qualified Interns for eLMS and jit.edu.ph | New |

**Discussion point:** 3 of 6 PI Objectives are still **New** — halfway through PI 6 (Iteration 6.4 of what appears to be a shorter PI), this suggests either delayed starts or objectives that haven't been decomposed into active work yet.

---

### 2. Features — 36 Items

| State | Count |
|---|---|
| Active | 15 |
| New | 17 |
| Requirements Gathering | 3 |
| Enabler Feature (New) | 1 |

**Key themes in the feature backlog:**
- **TESDA Compliance & Certification** — CSS NC II, Assessment Center, MCC applications (core regulatory work)
- **SAFe Training Offerings** — Leading SAFe, POPM, Scrum Master, SAFe for Teams, RTE (revenue-generating courses)
- **New Course Development** — Bubble.io, Markdown, Sitecore, ChatGPT, Prompt Engineering
- **Intern Programs** — St. Mary of Bansalan, UM CPE, HCDC, UIC, Cor Jesu College
- **OpenEduCat/ODOO** — LMS platform setup

**Red flags:**
- **7 Features are parked at the root iteration path** (not assigned to any PI) — these are orphaned work
- **1 Feature still in PI 4** (Create Markdown Language Courseware) — stale, never completed
- **No Effort/Business Value** set on any Feature — WSJF prioritization is impossible
- **47% of Features are New** — nearly half haven't started

---

### 3. Stories & Deliverables — 42 Items

| State | Count |
|---|---|
| New | 15 |
| Requirements Gathering | 4 |
| Grooming | 5 |
| Ready | 3 |
| Ready for Dev | 2 |
| Active | 2 |
| Validation | 1 |

**Work item type breakdown:**

| Type | Count | % |
|---|---|---|
| User Story | 17 | 40% |
| Courseware | 7 | 17% |
| Enabler | 6 | 14% |
| Spike | 3 | 7% |
| Training | 3 | 7% |

**Story Points:** Only **24 of 42 items** (57%) have story points. All User Stories in the current iteration have points (1–3 pts), but Coursewares, Enablers, and Trainings generally do not.

**Stale items from PI 3 (Jun–Sep 2025):**
- 7 Enablers (Google Cloud Training for Trainors) still in "New" — **8+ months old**
- 2 Spikes (AWS/Microsoft Educational Partnerships) still in "New"
- 1 Courseware (Intro to Rust) still in "New"

---

### 4. Iteration Health — Current Sprint (6.4)

Iteration 6.4 (Feb 23 – Mar 8) has **~18 stories** loaded, including:
- TESDA compliance tasks (multiple AC compliance documents)
- Intern onboarding (Cor Jesu College)
- CSS NC II certificate awarding
- Markdown training dry-run
- SK Buhangin partnership signing
- ChatGPT Courseware prep

Most of these are **New** — they were just added to this iteration and haven't started. With only 2 weeks in the sprint, this is a heavy load.

---

### 5. Team Workload Distribution

| Team Member | Items Assigned | % of Total |
|---|---|---|
| **armelita** | **57** | **68%** |
| grace | 13 | 15% |
| Samantha Babael | 4 | 5% |
| RAMON ASENIERO JR | 2 | 2% |
| Teofilo Limpag | 1 | 1% |
| Unassigned | 5 | 6% |

**This is the single biggest concern.** Armelita carries 68% of all work items across every backlog level. This creates a critical single point of failure.

---

### 6. Key Discussion Points

**A. Backlog Hygiene (Critical)**
- 10+ items from PI 3 (8 months ago) are still "New" — they should be either actively pursued, re-estimated, or removed
- 7 Features sit at the root iteration with no PI assignment
- Zero risks or impediments logged despite the heavy workload

**B. Workload Imbalance (Critical)**

**E. Work Item Type Proliferation (Minor)**

**F. Iteration Anomaly**

# SAFe Audit Report — Jairosoft Portfolio **Audit Date:** 2026-02-25 **Auditor:** Claude AI (Agile PM Consultant) **Framework:** Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) 6.0 **Organization:** Jairosoft LLC (`dev.azure.com/jairo`) **Project:** Jairosoft Portfolio --- ## 1. Executive Summary The Jairosoft Portfolio project is a multi-team, multi-product initiative managed through Azure DevOps with SAFe-aligned practices. The project spans 21 teams organized across Agile Release Trains (ARTs), with work structured through Program Increments (PIs). The portfolio currently operates across two primary product domains — **Colina Health** and **Sitecore** — alongside training, marketing, and operational functions delivered through the **Jairo Institute of Technology (JIT)**. ### Overall SAFe Maturity Assessment | Dimension | Score | Rating | |---|---|---| | Team Organization | 7/10 | Good | | PI Cadence & Iterations | 8/10 | Strong | | Backlog Hierarchy | 8/10 | Strong | | Work Item Discipline | 5/10 | Needs Improvement | | CI/CD Pipeline Health | 6/10 | Moderate | | Lean Portfolio Management | 7/10 | Good | **Overall Score: 6.8 / 10 — Moderate SAFe Maturity** --- ## 2. Organizational Structure The project has **21 teams**, which maps well to a SAFe Agile Release Train (ART) structure. ### Team Categories ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Portfolio Level" LPM["Jairosoft Lean Portfolio Management"] PM["Program Management Team"] end subgraph "Agile Teams (Development)" B["Bravo Agile Team"] W["Whiskey Agile Team"] U["Unicorn Agile Team"] C["Charlie Agile Team"] end subgraph "Product Teams" JIT["JIT Site eLMS Product Team"] CH["Colina Health Product Team"] MS["Marketing Site Product Team"] end subgraph "Shared Services" UX["UI UX Design Team"] DO["DevOps IT System Team"] AI["AI Coalition Team"] FO["FinOps SuccessForPeople Team"] MK["Marketing Sales Team"] TP["Technical Pre-Sales Team"] JO["JIT Operation Team"] JP["Jairosoft Project Team"] end subgraph "Intern Teams" IW["Interns Whiskey Team"] ID["Interns DevOps IT Team"] IU["Interns Unicorn Team"] IDe["Interns Design Team"] end LPM --> PM PM --> B & W & U & C PM --> JIT & CH & MS ``` ### Findings — Team Organization **Strengths:** - Clear SAFe hierarchy with a **Lean Portfolio Management** team at the top, which is a SAFe best practice for portfolio-level governance. - **Program Management Team** exists to coordinate across ARTs. - Named Agile teams (Bravo, Whiskey, Unicorn, Charlie) suggest structured team identity — a hallmark of healthy SAFe adoption. - Dedicated **Product Teams** for each value stream (JIT eLMS, Colina Health, Marketing Site). - **Shared Services** teams (DevOps IT, AI Coalition, UI/UX Design) properly support cross-cutting concerns. **Concerns:** - **21 teams is high** for a single ART. SAFe recommends 5–12 teams per ART. Consider whether this portfolio should be restructured into **2–3 ARTs** (e.g., Colina Health ART, JIT/Education ART, Marketing/Sales ART). - **4 Intern teams** may create coordination overhead. Consider embedding interns within established Agile teams rather than maintaining separate teams. - Several teams lack descriptions, making it hard to understand their scope and charter. --- ## 3. PI Cadence & Iteration Structure The portfolio follows a well-defined PI cadence with the following timeline: ### PI Timeline | PI | Start Date | End Date | Duration | Status | |---|---|---|---|---| | PI 0 | Sep 16, 2024 | Dec 29, 2024 | ~15 weeks | Completed | | PI 1 | Dec 30, 2024 | Mar 23, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 2 | Mar 24, 2025 | Jun 15, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 3 | Jun 16, 2025 | Sep 07, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 4 | Sep 08, 2025 | Nov 30, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 5 | Dec 01, 2025 | Feb 22, 2026 | ~12 weeks | **Just Completed** | ### PI 3 Iteration Breakdown (Detail Available) ```mermaid gantt title PI 3 Iteration Schedule dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD section PI 3 Iteration 3.1 :2025-06-16, 2025-06-29 Iteration 3.2 :2025-06-30, 2025-07-13 Iteration 3.3 :2025-07-14, 2025-07-27 Iteration 3.4 :2025-07-28, 2025-08-10 Iteration 3.5 :2025-08-11, 2025-08-24 Iteration 3.6 (IP) :2025-08-25, 2025-09-07 ``` ### Findings — PI Cadence **Strengths:** - Consistent **12-week PI cadence** from PI 1 onward (PI 0 was a 15-week onboarding/setup PI, which is acceptable). - PI 3 shows proper **2-week iteration cadence** with 5 development iterations + 1 Innovation & Planning (IP) iteration — this is textbook SAFe. - The IP iteration (3.6) is correctly labeled and positioned at the end of the PI. - **6 PIs completed** in ~17 months demonstrates commitment and velocity. **Concerns:** - As of Feb 25, 2026, PI 5 just ended (Feb 22). No PI 6 iteration structure is visible — this suggests **PI Planning may be delayed** or hasn't been configured yet in ADO. - PI 0 was 3 weeks longer than subsequent PIs. While acceptable for a launch PI, the variance should be documented. - Only PI 3 has visible sub-iterations; PIs 0, 1, 2, 4, and 5 do not show iteration breakdowns at the portfolio level. **Recommendation:** Ensure PI 6 planning is scheduled immediately. The 3-day gap between PIs should be used for Inspect & Adapt (I&A) and PI Planning events. --- ## 4. Backlog Hierarchy & Work Item Types The portfolio uses a customized SAFe backlog hierarchy: ```mermaid graph TD L5["Goals & Releases
(Goal, Release)"] L4["Epics & Objectives
(Epic, PI Objective)"] L3["Features & Enablers
(Feature, Enabler Feature, Change Request)"] L2["Stories & Deliverables
(User Story, Enabler, Spike, Defect,
Courseware, Training, Design, Tech Debt)"] L1["Tasks
(Task, Peer Review)"] L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1 style L5 fill:#60af49,color:#fff style L4 fill:#E06C00,color:#fff style L3 fill:#773B93,color:#fff style L2 fill:#0098C7,color:#fff style L1 fill:#A4880A,color:#fff ``` ### Findings — Backlog Hierarchy **Strengths:** - **5-level hierarchy** aligns well with SAFe's portfolio, large solution, program, and team levels. - Custom work item types like **Courseware**, **Training**, and **Design** show thoughtful adaptation of SAFe to the organization's domain (education/training). - **PI Objective** as a work item type at the Epic level is a strong SAFe practice. - **Risks and Impediments** backlog (Issue, Risk) is defined — this supports ROAM risk management. - **Peer Review** as a task type promotes quality practices. **Concerns:** - The **Features and Enablers** and **Stories and Deliverables** backlogs are marked as **hidden** (`isHidden: true`). # SAFe Audit Report — Jairosoft Portfolio **Audit Date:** 2026-02-25 **Auditor:** Claude AI (Agile PM Consultant) **Framework:** Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) 6.0 **Organization:** Jairosoft LLC (`dev.azure.com/jairo`) **Project:** Jairosoft Portfolio --- ## 1. Executive Summary The Jairosoft Portfolio project is a multi-team, multi-product initiative managed through Azure DevOps with SAFe-aligned practices. The project spans 21 teams organized across Agile Release Trains (ARTs), with work structured through Program Increments (PIs). The portfolio currently operates across two primary product domains — **Colina Health** and **Sitecore** — alongside training, marketing, and operational functions delivered through the **Jairo Institute of Technology (JIT)**. ### Overall SAFe Maturity Assessment | Dimension | Score | Rating | |---|---|---| | Team Organization | 7/10 | Good | | PI Cadence & Iterations | 8/10 | Strong | | Backlog Hierarchy | 8/10 | Strong | | Work Item Discipline | 5/10 | Needs Improvement | | CI/CD Pipeline Health | 6/10 | Moderate | | Lean Portfolio Management | 7/10 | Good | **Overall Score: 6.8 / 10 — Moderate SAFe Maturity** --- ## 2. Organizational Structure The project has **21 teams**, which maps well to a SAFe Agile Release Train (ART) structure. ### Team Categories ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Portfolio Level" LPM["Jairosoft Lean Portfolio Management"] PM["Program Management Team"] end subgraph "Agile Teams (Development)" B["Bravo Agile Team"] W["Whiskey Agile Team"] U["Unicorn Agile Team"] C["Charlie Agile Team"] end subgraph "Product Teams" JIT["JIT Site eLMS Product Team"] CH["Colina Health Product Team"] MS["Marketing Site Product Team"] end subgraph "Shared Services" UX["UI UX Design Team"] DO["DevOps IT System Team"] AI["AI Coalition Team"] FO["FinOps SuccessForPeople Team"] MK["Marketing Sales Team"] TP["Technical Pre-Sales Team"] JO["JIT Operation Team"] JP["Jairosoft Project Team"] end subgraph "Intern Teams" IW["Interns Whiskey Team"] ID["Interns DevOps IT Team"] IU["Interns Unicorn Team"] IDe["Interns Design Team"] end LPM --> PM PM --> B & W & U & C PM --> JIT & CH & MS ``` ### Findings — Team Organization **Strengths:** - Clear SAFe hierarchy with a **Lean Portfolio Management** team at the top, which is a SAFe best practice for portfolio-level governance. - **Program Management Team** exists to coordinate across ARTs. - Named Agile teams (Bravo, Whiskey, Unicorn, Charlie) suggest structured team identity — a hallmark of healthy SAFe adoption. - Dedicated **Product Teams** for each value stream (JIT eLMS, Colina Health, Marketing Site). - **Shared Services** teams (DevOps IT, AI Coalition, UI/UX Design) properly support cross-cutting concerns. **Concerns:** - **21 teams is high** for a single ART. SAFe recommends 5–12 teams per ART. Consider whether this portfolio should be restructured into **2–3 ARTs** (e.g., Colina Health ART, JIT/Education ART, Marketing/Sales ART). - **4 Intern teams** may create coordination overhead. Consider embedding interns within established Agile teams rather than maintaining separate teams. - Several teams lack descriptions, making it hard to understand their scope and charter. --- ## 3. PI Cadence & Iteration Structure The portfolio follows a well-defined PI cadence with the following timeline: ### PI Timeline | PI | Start Date | End Date | Duration | Status | |---|---|---|---|---| | PI 0 | Sep 16, 2024 | Dec 29, 2024 | ~15 weeks | Completed | | PI 1 | Dec 30, 2024 | Mar 23, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 2 | Mar 24, 2025 | Jun 15, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 3 | Jun 16, 2025 | Sep 07, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 4 | Sep 08, 2025 | Nov 30, 2025 | ~12 weeks | Completed | | PI 5 | Dec 01, 2025 | Feb 22, 2026 | ~12 weeks | **Just Completed** | ### PI 3 Iteration Breakdown (Detail Available) ```mermaid gantt title PI 3 Iteration Schedule dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD section PI 3 Iteration 3.1 :2025-06-16, 2025-06-29 Iteration 3.2 :2025-06-30, 2025-07-13 Iteration 3.3 :2025-07-14, 2025-07-27 Iteration 3.4 :2025-07-28, 2025-08-10 Iteration 3.5 :2025-08-11, 2025-08-24 Iteration 3.6 (IP) :2025-08-25, 2025-09-07 ``` ### Findings — PI Cadence **Strengths:** - Consistent **12-week PI cadence** from PI 1 onward (PI 0 was a 15-week onboarding/setup PI, which is acceptable). - PI 3 shows proper **2-week iteration cadence** with 5 development iterations + 1 Innovation & Planning (IP) iteration — this is textbook SAFe. - The IP iteration (3.6) is correctly labeled and positioned at the end of the PI. - **6 PIs completed** in ~17 months demonstrates commitment and velocity. **Concerns:** - As of Feb 25, 2026, PI 5 just ended (Feb 22). No PI 6 iteration structure is visible — this suggests **PI Planning may be delayed** or hasn't been configured yet in ADO. - PI 0 was 3 weeks longer than subsequent PIs. While acceptable for a launch PI, the variance should be documented. - Only PI 3 has visible sub-iterations; PIs 0, 1, 2, 4, and 5 do not show iteration breakdowns at the portfolio level. **Recommendation:** Ensure PI 6 planning is scheduled immediately. The 3-day gap between PIs should be used for Inspect & Adapt (I&A) and PI Planning events. --- ## 4. Backlog Hierarchy & Work Item Types The portfolio uses a customized SAFe backlog hierarchy: ```mermaid graph TD L5["Goals & Releases
(Goal, Release)"] L4["Epics & Objectives
(Epic, PI Objective)"] L3["Features & Enablers
(Feature, Enabler Feature, Change Request)"] L2["Stories & Deliverables
(User Story, Enabler, Spike, Defect,
Courseware, Training, Design, Tech Debt)"] L1["Tasks
(Task, Peer Review)"] L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1 style L5 fill:#60af49,color:#fff style L4 fill:#E06C00,color:#fff style L3 fill:#773B93,color:#fff style L2 fill:#0098C7,color:#fff style L1 fill:#A4880A,color:#fff ``` ### Findings — Backlog Hierarchy **Strengths:** - **5-level hierarchy** aligns well with SAFe's portfolio, large solution, program, and team levels. - Custom work item types like **Courseware**, **Training**, and **Design** show thoughtful adaptation of SAFe to the organization's domain (education/training). - **PI Objective** as a work item type at the Epic level is a strong SAFe practice. - **Risks and Impediments** backlog (Issue, Risk) is defined — this supports ROAM risk management. - **Peer Review** as a task type promotes quality practices. **Concerns:** - The **Features and Enablers** and **Stories and Deliverables** backlogs are marked as **hidden** (`isHidden: true`). This could mean teams aren't using the board views at these levels, losing visibility. - The **Risks and Impediments** backlog is also hidden — risk management visibility may be lacking. - **8 work item types** at the Story level is high. Consider whether Spike, Courseware, Training, and Design could be consolidated or tracked via tags instead. --- ## 5. Work Item Health Analysis Based on the 50 sampled work items: ### State Distribution | State | Count | % | |---|---|---| | Closed | 30 | 60% | | New | 8 | 16% | | Blocked | 3 | 6% | | Removed | 3 | 6% | | Requirements Gathering | 4 | 8% | | Grooming | 1 | 2% | | Other | 1 | 2% | ### Work Item Type Distribution ```mermaid pie title Work Item Types (Sampled) "Spike" : 21 "Enabler" : 8 "Training" : 4 "Courseware" : 5 "User Story" : 3 "Task" : 7 "Other" : 2 ``` ### Key Team Members | Team Member | Work Items | Primary Types | |---|---|---| | armelita@jairosoft.com | 13 | Training, Enabler, Courseware | | Jeffrey Avila | 7 | Spike (Marketing) | | Roche Casipong | 7 | Spike (Registration/Sales) | | grace@jairosoft.com | 5 | User Story, Courseware | | Elmer Joseph Cutin | 3 | Spike (Marketing) | | Vicsante Aseniero | 3 | Spike, Enabler | | Ramon Aseniero | 2 | Spike, User Story | ### Findings — Work Items **Strengths:** - **60% closure rate** on sampled items shows work is getting done. - Clear ownership — most work items have assigned team members. - Work spans multiple domains: marketing, training/courseware development, product development (Colina Health), and operations. **Critical Concerns:** - **Spikes dominate at 42%** of all work items. SAFe recommends spikes as time-boxed research activities, not the primary work type. This suggests the organization may be over-using Spikes instead of proper User Stories with acceptance criteria. - **User Stories are only 6%** of sampled work — this is alarmingly low. Value delivery should be primarily tracked through User Stories. - **3 Blocked items (6%)** with no visible resolution path. Blocked items tagged "OnHold" suggest systemic impediments that aren't being actively resolved. - **3 Removed items (6%)** indicate scope instability or poor backlog refinement. - **Items in "Requirements Gathering" and "Grooming" states** suggest some work items are languishing in pre-development stages. - **No Story Points visible** on any sampled work items — velocity tracking may not be active. --- ## 6. CI/CD Pipeline Health ### Pipeline Inventory (13 Pipelines) | Pipeline | Domain | Last Build | Status | |---|---|---|---| | BEColinaHealth.git Backend CICD | Colina Health | Feb 9, 2026 | Success | | colinahealth.git FrontEnd CICD | Colina Health | Feb 5, 2026 | Success | | Colina BE Docker Image | Colina Health | Mar 10, 2025 | Success | | Colina FE Docker Image | Colina Health | Mar 11, 2025 | Success | | ColinaHealth-API_Golang_Build | Colina Health | Mar 6, 2025 | Success | | colinahealth.git | Colina Health | Feb 5, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore Environment Creation | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore Environment Deletion | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | **Partial Failure** | | Sitecore-CD Repository Creation | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore-CM Repository Creation | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore Repository Deletion | Sitecore | Aug 14, 2025 | Success | | Sitecore | Sitecore | Jul 9, 2025 | Success | | SugConPH | Sitecore | Aug 15, 2025 | Success | ### Pipeline Activity Timeline ```mermaid gantt title Pipeline Last Build Activity dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD section Active (2026) BE Backend CICD :done, 2026-02-09, 1d FE Frontend CICD :done, 2026-02-05, 1d section Stale (2025) Colina BE Docker :crit, 2025-03-10, 1d Colina FE Docker :crit, 2025-03-11, 1d ColinaHealth-API Go :crit, 2025-03-06, 1d colinahealth.git :crit, 2025-02-05, 1d Sitecore Env Create :crit, 2025-08-14, 1d Sitecore Env Delete :crit, 2025-08-14, 1d Sitecore :crit, 2025-07-09, 1d SugConPH :crit, 2025-08-15, 1d ``` ### Findings — CI/CD **Strengths:** - **13 pipelines** across two product domains shows CI/CD investment. - The two most recent builds (Feb 2026) are for Colina Health, indicating active development on that product. - All builds except one show successful status. **Concerns:** - **11 of 13 pipelines haven't been triggered since 2025** — this means 85% of pipelines are stale. Either the codebases are dormant or builds aren't being triggered regularly. - **Sitecore pipelines** last ran in Aug 2025 (6+ months ago) — if Sitecore is still an active product, this is a red flag for continuous integration practices. - The **Sitecore Environment Deletion** pipeline shows a partial failure that was never addressed. - Only **Colina Health Backend and Frontend** pipelines show recent (2026) activity, suggesting development effort is concentrated on a single product. --- ## 7. Repository Analysis ### Repository Inventory (9 Repos) | Repository | Size | Domain | |---|---|---| | Sitecore-CD | 236 MB | Sitecore | | Sitecore-CM | 187 MB | Sitecore | | BEColinaHealth-GO | 42 MB | Colina Health | | SugConPH | 31 MB | Sitecore | | colinahealth.git | 30 MB | Colina Health | | FEColinaHealth-GO | 19 MB | Colina Health | | BEColinaHealth.git | 3.5 MB | Colina Health | | Sitecore | 2.6 MB | Sitecore | | JairoDevOps | <1 KB | DevOps | ### Findings — Repositories - **Sitecore repos are the largest** (423 MB combined) but show no recent pipeline activity — potential tech debt accumulation. - **Colina Health** has both legacy (.git suffix) and newer (GO suffix) repos, suggesting a technology migration from one stack to Go. - **JairoDevOps** repo is essentially empty (723 bytes) — should be cleaned up or documented. --- ## 8. SAFe Compliance Assessment ### SAFe Core Values Alignment | SAFe Core Value | Status | Evidence | |---|---|---| | **Alignment** | Partial | PI cadence exists; PI Objectives are defined as work item types. However, no visible Strategic Themes or portfolio Kanban flow. | | **Built-in Quality** | Needs Improvement | Peer Review task type exists, but no evidence of Definition of Done, automated testing metrics, or code review policies. | | **Transparency** | Partial | Work items are tracked, but key backlogs (Features, Stories, Risks) are hidden from board views. | | **Program Execution** | Good | 6 PIs completed with consistent cadence. Teams are structured and work is assigned. | ### SAFe Essential Practices Checklist | Practice | Implemented? | Notes | |---|---|---| | PI Planning | Likely | PI structure exists, but no formal PI Planning artifacts visible | | Iteration Planning | Partial | Only PI 3 shows sub-iterations | | Iteration Review/Demo | Unknown | No evidence in ADO | | Iteration Retrospective | Unknown | No evidence in ADO | | PI System Demo | Unknown | No evidence in ADO | | Inspect & Adapt | Unknown | No formal I&A artifacts visible | | Backlog Refinement | Partial | Items in "Grooming" state suggest some refinement | | Continuous Integration | Partial | Pipelines exist but most are stale | | Continuous Deployment | Partial | Docker-based pipelines suggest CD capability for Colina Health | | PI Objectives | Yes | PI Objective work item type defined at Epic level | | WSJF Prioritization | Unknown | No business value or time criticality data visible on items | --- ## 9. Recommendations ### Immediate Actions (This PI) 1. **Plan PI 6 immediately.** PI 5 ended Feb 22 — schedule PI Planning within the next week to avoid losing momentum. 2. **Address 3 Blocked work items.** Conduct an impediment removal session; items tagged "OnHold" need executive escalation or formal removal. 3. **Unhide Feature and Story backlogs.** Teams need board-level visibility at these levels for proper flow management. ### Short-Term (Next 1–2 PIs) 4. **Reduce Spike usage.** Convert ongoing research activities into proper User Stories with acceptance criteria. Target <15% Spikes in the backlog. 5. **Implement Story Points.** Without velocity data, predictability and capacity planning are impossible. 6. **Reactivate or archive stale pipelines.** 11 dormant pipelines create confusion; either trigger regular builds or formally decommission them. 7. **Restructure into 2–3 ARTs.** With 21 teams, consider splitting into Colina Health ART, JIT/Education ART, and Marketing/Operations ART. ### Long-Term (Next 2–4 PIs) 8. **Implement WSJF prioritization.** Add Business Value and Time Criticality fields to Features and Enablers. 9. **Embed interns into existing teams.** Reduce the 4 separate intern teams to improve mentoring and reduce coordination overhead. 10. **Establish Inspect & Adapt cadence.** Formalize I&A ceremonies at the end of each PI with documented improvement backlog. 11. **Clean up repository landscape.** Archive or document the empty JairoDevOps repo; assess whether legacy Colina Health repos (non-GO) should be deprecated. --- ## 10. # SAFe Iteration Audit Report — JIT Operation Team, Iteration 6.4

**Audit Date:** 2026-02-24
**Auditor:** Claude AI (Agile PM Consultant, 10+ years SAFe experience)
**Framework:** Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) 6.0
**Organization:** Jairosoft LLC (`dev.azure.com/jairo`)
**Project:** Jairosoft Portfolio
**Team:** JIT Operation Team
**Scope:** Iteration 6.4 (February 23 – March 8, 2026)
**PI Context:** 2026-PI6, Final Iteration (4 of 4)

---

## 1. Executive Summary

This audit examines the **JIT Operation Team's** board and current sprint (Iteration 6.4) within the Jairosoft Portfolio project. The JIT Operation Team is classified as a **Shared Services** team responsible for training operations, TESDA certification/compliance, courseware development, intern programs, and partnerships for the Jairo Institute of Technology.

The team is in **Day 2 of a 14-day sprint** with 17 work items committed totaling 23 story points. While the sprint demonstrates strong fundamentals — story-driven delivery, proper estimation, and right-sized work items — critical process gaps in capacity planning, workload balance, and risk management severely undermine execution predictability.

### Overall Iteration Health

| Dimension | Score | Rating |
|---|---|---|
| Capacity Planning | 1/10 | Critical |
| Workload Balance | 2/10 | Critical |
| Risk Management | 1/10 | Critical |
| State Discipline | 4/10 | Poor |
| Sprint Scope | 5/10 | Moderate |
| Estimation Quality | 7/10 | Good |
| Work Item Types | 8/10 | Strong |
| Story Point Adoption | 8/10 | Strong |

**Overall Iteration Score: 4.5 / 10 — Needs Significant Improvement**

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Iteration 6.4 Sprint Health — JIT Operation Team
    x-axis Low Health --> High Health
    y-axis Low Risk --> High Risk
    quadrant-1 Fix Immediately
    quadrant-2 Monitor Closely
    quadrant-3 Acceptable
    quadrant-4 Improve Next Sprint
    "Capacity Planning": [0.05, 0.90]
    "Workload Balance": [0.15, 0.85]
    "Story Point Adoption": [0.80, 0.20]
    "Work Item Types": [0.75, 0.25]
    "State Discipline": [0.30, 0.70]
    "Risk Management": [0.10, 0.65]
    "Sprint Scope": [0.45, 0.55]
    "Estimation Quality": [0.70, 0.30]
```

---

## 2. Team Profile

### 2.1 Team Roster (Azure DevOps)

The JIT Operation Team has **2 registered members** in Azure DevOps:

| Member | Email | Registered | Capacity (Iteration 6.4) |
|---|---|---|---|
| armelita | armelita@jairosoft.com | Yes | **0 hrs/day** |
| grace | grace@jairosoft.com | Yes | **0 hrs/day** |

**Additional contributors** (not registered as team members):

| Contributor | Items in Sprint | Registered |
|---|---|---|
| Samantha Babael | 3 | No |
| Teofilo Limpag | 1 | No |

**Total team capacity configured: 0 hrs/day**

### 2.2 Board Configuration

The team's board is configured with **6 backlog levels**:

| Backlog Level | Work Item Types | Visible | Items |
|---|---|---|---|
| Goals and Releases | Goal, Release | Hidden | -- |
| Epics and Objectives | Epic, PI Objective | Visible | 6 |
| Features and Enablers | Feature, Enabler Feature, Change Request | Visible | 36 |
| Stories and Deliverables | User Story, Enabler, Spike, Courseware, Training, Design, Defect, Tech Debt | Visible | 42 |
| Risks and Impediments | Issue, Risk | Visible | **0** |
| Tasks | Task, Peer Review, Bug | Visible | -- |

**Total board work items: 84**

---

## 3. PI 6 Context — PI Objectives

The JIT Operation Team has **6 PI Objectives** defined for 2026-PI6. All are typed as "PI Objective" at the Epic level — a correct SAFe practice.

| ID | PI Objective | State | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|
| 197441 | Produce Professional Alumni | Active | armelita |
| 197443 | Recognized as CSS NC II Assessment Center | Active | armelita |
| 197445 | Launch new courses | Active | armelita |
| 197444 | CSS NC II Assessment Offering | New | Unassigned |
| 197446 | Use OpenEduCat for JIT Operations | New | armelita |
| 197450 | Onboard Qualified Interns for eLMS and jit.edu.ph | New | armelita |

```mermaid
pie title PI 6 Objective States
    "Active" : 3
    "New" : 3
```

**Finding:** In the **final iteration of PI 6**, 3 of 6 PI Objectives (50%) remain in "New" state. Per SAFe, PI Objectives should be actively worked toward throughout the PI. Objectives still in "New" in the last iteration suggest either:
- The objectives were not properly decomposed into Features/Stories
- Work started but the objective status was never updated
- The objectives are at risk of not being met this PI

**Recommendation:** Before PI 6 closes, update all PI Objective states to reflect reality. During the upcoming Inspect & Adapt, formally assess which objectives were met vs. missed.

---

## 4. Iteration Structure — PI 6 Timeline

The JIT Operation Team is subscribed to iterations spanning PI 3 through PI 6:

### PI 6 Iteration Schedule

```mermaid
gantt
    title 2026-PI6 — JIT Operation Team Iterations
    dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD
    section PI 6
    Iteration 6.1 (Completed)   :done, 2026-01-12, 2026-01-25
    Iteration 6.2 (Completed)   :done, 2026-01-26, 2026-02-08
    Iteration 6.3 (Completed)   :done, 2026-02-09, 2026-02-22
    Iteration 6.4 (CURRENT)     :active, 2026-02-23, 2026-03-08
```

| Iteration | Start | End | Duration | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 6.1 | Jan 12, 2026 | Jan 25, 2026 | 2 weeks | Completed |
| 6.2 | Jan 26, 2026 | Feb 08, 2026 | 2 weeks | Completed |
| 6.3 | Feb 09, 2026 | Feb 22, 2026 | 2 weeks | Completed |
| **6.4** | **Feb 23, 2026** | **Mar 08, 2026** | **2 weeks** | **Current** |

**Finding:** PI 6 has **4 iterations** (8 weeks) instead of the standard SAFe 5+1 IP pattern (12 weeks). Earlier PIs (PI 3, PI 4) had 5–6 iterations each. This shortened PI may indicate:
- A deliberate cadence change
- Incomplete iteration configuration
- Missing Innovation & Planning (IP) iteration

**SAFe Note:** The absence of a dedicated IP iteration means no protected time for innovation, planning, and Inspect & Adapt. This should be addressed for PI 7.

---

## 5. Iteration 6.4 — Sprint Backlog Audit

### 5.1 Sprint Metrics Summary

| Items "Active" | 1 of 17 (6%) | 20–40% | **Stalled** |

| 2 | 197617 | User Story | Signing of Agreement on SK Buhangin Partnership | Ready for Dev | armelita | 1 |
| 3 | 198612 | User Story | Follow up Sam Application as Trainer | Ready for Dev | armelita | 1 |
| 4 | 198630 | Training | Markdown Training for the Employees | Ready | Samantha Babael | -- |
| 5 | 199221 | Courseware | ChatGPT Courseware | Ready | Samantha Babael | -- |
| 6 | 198637 | User Story | Markdown Training Dry-run | **Grooming** | Samantha Babael | 1 |
| 7 | 198615 | User Story | Awarding of CSS NC II Certificates | New | armelita | 2 |

### 5.3 Work Item State Distribution

```mermaid
pie title Iteration 6.4 — Work Item States (Day 2)
    "New" : 10
    "Ready for Dev" : 2
    "Ready" : 2
    "Grooming" : 1
    "Active" : 1
```

**Analysis:**
- **59% New** — The majority of sprint work has not entered the team's workflow. On Day 2 of a 14-day sprint, this signals either late sprint planning or work items that were added without proper commitment.
- **12% Ready for Dev** — 2 items are refined and ready to be picked up.
- **12% Ready** — 2 items (Training + Courseware) are prepped but haven't been pulled.
- **6% Grooming** — 1 item is still being refined inside the sprint. **This is a SAFe violation** — items should meet Definition of Ready before entering an iteration.
- **6% Active** — Only 1 item has actual work in progress.

### 5.4 Work Item Type Distribution

**Positive Finding:** User Stories account for **82% of the sprint**. This is a major improvement over the portfolio-wide observation where Spikes dominated at 42% and User Stories were only 6%. The JIT Operation Team is properly using User Stories as the primary vehicle for value delivery — a SAFe best practice.

## 6. Workload Distribution Analysis

### 6.3 Finding — Critical Workload Imbalance

**armelita carries 76% of all sprint work (13 items, 18 of 23 story points).** This is the single most critical finding in this audit.

| Risk Factor | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Bus Factor** | If armelita is unavailable for even 2–3 days, 76% of the sprint is at risk |
| **Bottleneck** | All 10 "New" items are hers — nothing moves until she starts them |
| **Burnout Risk** | 18 story points in 10 working days = ~1.8 pts/day sustained |
| **Quality Risk** | High volume + single person = reduced time for thorough work |

**SAFe Guidance:** SAFe recommends work be distributed across team members to promote collective ownership and reduce dependency risk. No single team member should own more than 30–40% of a sprint's committed work.

| 3 | **Clarify TESDA AC compliance deadline** — if there is a hard regulatory date, the 7 compliance items (IDs: 199496–199503) must be prioritized above all other work | armelita | High |
| 4 | **Resolve item 198637** (Markdown Training Dry-run) — complete grooming and move to "Ready" or defer to next sprint | Samantha Babael | Medium |
| 5 | **Estimate 2 unestimated items** (198630 Markdown Training, 199221 ChatGPT Courseware) | Team | Medium |
| 6 | **Move "New" items to "Active"** as work begins — update board states daily to reflect actual progress | All team members | Medium |

|---|---|---|---|

| 9 | **Conduct Iteration Review** — demo completed work to stakeholders, especially TESDA compliance deliverables | Team | Medium |
| 10 | **Conduct Iteration Retrospective** — discuss: Why is capacity not configured? Is the workload distribution sustainable? Are items entering sprints in "Ready" state? | Team | Medium |

### 13.3 For PI 7 Planning

| # | Action | Owner | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| 11 | **Redistribute workload** — target no single team member owning more than 40% of sprint items | Scrum Master | High |
| 12 | **Conduct backlog grooming** — review and remove/re-plan 20 stale items from PI 3 and root iteration | Team | High |
| 13 | **Include an IP iteration** in PI 7 — protect time for innovation, hackathons, and Inspect & Adapt | RTE / Program Manager | High |
| 14 | **Populate Effort and Business Value** on all Features to enable WSJF prioritization | Product Owner | Medium |
| 15 | **Start logging risks** in the Risks and Impediments backlog — use ROAM categorization | Team | Medium |
| 16 | **Establish velocity baseline** — use Iteration 6.4 actuals + first 2 sprints of PI 7 to calculate average velocity for future planning | Scrum Master | Medium |

### 13.4 Recommendation Priority Matrix

    title Recommendation Priority — Effort vs Impact
    x-axis Low Effort --> High Effort
    y-axis Low Impact --> High Impact
    quadrant-1 Plan & Schedule
    quadrant-2 Do First
    quadrant-3 Defer
    quadrant-4 Quick Wins
    "Configure Capacity": [0.15, 0.90]

    "Backlog Grooming (Stale)": [0.55, 0.65]
    "Populate WSJF Fields": [0.70, 0.60]
    "Add IP Iteration to PI 7": [0.40, 0.70]
    "Start ROAM Risk Tracking": [0.30, 0.55]
```

---

## 14. Conclusion

The JIT Operation Team demonstrates **promising SAFe fundamentals** — properly typed PI Objectives, story-driven delivery (82% User Stories), reasonable story point adoption (88%), and right-sized work items (1–3 points). These are signs of a team that understands the mechanics of agile delivery.

However, **critical process gaps** prevent the team from realizing the benefits of these fundamentals:

1. **Zero configured capacity** makes planning and tracking meaningless
2. **76% workload concentration on one person** creates unacceptable organizational risk
3. **No risk management** leaves the team blind to impediments
4. **Stale backlog items** (8+ months) indicate insufficient refinement discipline
5. **No IP iteration** removes protected time for improvement

The path forward is clear: **fix capacity and workload balance first** (Quick Wins), then **establish velocity and refinement cadence** (Short Term), and finally **implement WSJF and formalize I&A** (Medium Term). The team has the foundation — it now needs the discipline.

---

Based on SAFe 6.0 framework standards from [ScaledAgileFramework.com](https://ScaledAgileFramework.com).*

The JIT Operation Team is properly using User Stories as the primary vehicle for value delivery — a SAFe best practice.

---

## 6. Workload Distribution Analysis

### 6.1 Load by Team Member

```mermaid

| Samantha Babael | 3 | 2 pts | 18% | 1 Ready, 1 Grooming, 1 Ready |
| Teofilo Limpag | 1 | 2 pts | 6% | 1 Active |
| **Total** | **17** | **23 pts** | **100%** | |

### 6.2 Workload Imbalance Visualization

```

### 6.3 Finding — Critical Workload Imbalance

**armelita carries 76% of all sprint work (13 items, 18 of 23 story points).** This is the single most critical finding in this audit.

| Risk Factor | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Bus Factor** | If armelita is unavailable for even 2–3 days, 76% of the sprint is at risk |
| **Bottleneck** | All 10 "New" items are hers — nothing moves until she starts them |
| **Burnout Risk** | 18 story points in 10 working days = ~1.8 pts/day sustained |
| **Quality Risk** | High volume + single person = reduced time for thorough work |

**SAFe Guidance:** SAFe recommends work be distributed across team members to promote collective ownership and reduce dependency risk. No single team member should own more than 30–40% of a sprint's committed work.

---

## 7. Sprint Theme Analysis

The 17 work items cluster into **4 strategic themes**:

| Certification & Reporting | 3 (18%) | 6 | Mixed | Medium |

**Key Insight:** The sprint is dominated by **TESDA Assessment Center compliance** (41% of items). These 7 items form a dependency cluster — they all relate to preparing documentation for regulatory compliance. If there is a hard TESDA deadline, these should be prioritized above all other work. Currently, all 7 are in "New" state on Day 2.

---

## 8. Capacity Analysis

### 8.1 Configured Capacity

| Member | Capacity/Day | Days Off | Role |

| armelita | **0 hrs** | 0 | Registered member |

### 8.2 Cross-Team Comparison (Iteration 6.4)

| Team | Capacity/Day | Days Off |
|---|---|---|
| Colina Health Product Team | 14.5 hrs | 2 |
| JIT Site eLMS Product Team | 48 hrs | 0 |
| **JIT Operation Team** | **0 hrs** | **0** |

**Finding:** The JIT Operation Team is the **only team with zero configured capacity** among the teams active in Iteration 6.4. Other teams (Colina Health at 14.5 hrs/day, JIT eLMS at 48 hrs/day) have properly configured capacity, demonstrating that the organization knows how to set this up — the JIT Operation Team simply hasn't done it.

### 8.3 Impact of Zero Capacity

Without configured capacity:
1. **Burndown charts** show no baseline — the team cannot track progress visually
2. **Sprint commitment validation** is impossible — there is no way to know if 23 points is achievable
3. **Azure DevOps capacity alerts** will not trigger — the system cannot warn about over-allocation
4. **Velocity tracking** has no anchor — historical capacity-to-delivery ratios cannot be calculated

---

This means **Weighted Shortest Job First (WSJF) prioritization is impossible** — a core SAFe Lean Portfolio Management practice.

---

## 10. State Flow & Process Compliance

### 10.1 Workflow States Observed

The team uses the following workflow states:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    Grooming --> Ready : Ready
