```mermaid
    ReadyForDev --> Active : Start
    Active --> Validation : Complete
    Validation --> Closed : Accept

    state "New" as New
    state "Requirements Gathering" as RequirementsGathering
    state "Grooming" as Grooming
    state "Ready" as Ready
    state "Ready for Dev" as ReadyForDev
    state "Active" as Active
    state "Validation" as Validation
    state "Closed" as Closed
```

### 10.2 SAFe Process Violations

| Violation | Item(s) | SAFe Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Item in "Grooming" inside active sprint | 198637 (Markdown Training Dry-run) | Items must meet Definition of Ready before entering an iteration |
| PI Objectives still "New" in final iteration | 197444, 197446, 197450 | PI Objectives should be actively tracked throughout the PI |
| Zero risks logged | Entire board | Teams should track risks using ROAM (Resolved, Owned, Accepted, Mitigated) |
| No capacity configured | All team members | Capacity must be set for burndown and commitment validation |
| Stale items from prior PIs | 10 items from PI 3 | Backlog refinement should clean up stale items each PI |

---

## 11. Risk Register

| # | Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Category | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **R1** | **Single point of failure** — armelita owns 76% of sprint work | High | Critical | People | Redistribute work; cross-train Samantha and Teofilo; add team members |
| **R2** | **Zero capacity configured** — sprint commitment is unvalidated | Confirmed | High | Process | Configure capacity for armelita, Samantha, and Teofilo immediately |
| **R3** | **TESDA AC Compliance deadline risk** — 7 regulatory items all "New" on Day 2 | Medium | High | Regulatory | Clarify TESDA deadline; if time-bound, prioritize above all other sprint work |
| **R4** | **PI Objectives at risk** — 50% still "New" in final iteration | High | Medium | Delivery | Assess PI Objective achievability; document unmet objectives for I&A |
| **R5** | **Unregistered team members** — Samantha and Teofilo not in ADO roster | Confirmed | Medium | Governance | Add them to the JIT Operation Team in ADO project settings |
| **R6** | **Backlog debt** — 20 stale items (PI 3 + root) creating noise | Confirmed | Low | Process | Conduct backlog refinement session; remove or re-plan stale items |
| **R7** | **No velocity baseline** — team cannot forecast future sprints | Confirmed | Medium | Planning | Track Iteration 6.4 actuals to establish first velocity data point |
| **R8** | **No IP iteration in PI 6** — no protected time for innovation or I&A | Confirmed | Medium | SAFe Compliance | Plan IP iteration for PI 7; schedule I&A ceremony at PI 6 close |

## 12. SAFe Compliance Assessment

### 12.1 SAFe Core Values

| Core Value | Status | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| **Alignment** | Partial | PI Objectives exist and are typed correctly. However, 50% are "New" in the final iteration, suggesting weak traceability from objectives to sprint work. |
| **Built-in Quality** | Unknown | No evidence of Definition of Done, acceptance criteria, or peer review tasks in the sprint backlog. |
| **Transparency** | Poor | Capacity = 0 makes burndown invisible. Risks/Impediments backlog is empty. Stale items obscure true backlog health. |
| **Program Execution** | Partial | Iteration cadence is maintained (2-week sprints). But work isn't flowing — 59% still "New" on Day 2. |

### 12.2 SAFe Essential Practices

| Practice | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Iteration Planning | Partial | Items assigned to sprint, but capacity not set and many items are "New" — suggests items were added without a formal planning ceremony |
| Definition of Ready | Not Enforced | 1 item in "Grooming" state is in the sprint |
| Capacity-Based Planning | Not Implemented | 0 hrs/day configured |
| WIP Limits | Not Visible | No evidence of WIP limits on the board |
| Daily Stand-up | Unknown | Ceremonies are not tracked in ADO |
| Iteration Review/Demo | Unknown | Sprint ends Mar 8 — no evidence of scheduling |
| Iteration Retrospective | Unknown | No evidence in ADO |
| Story Point Estimation | Mostly Implemented | 88% estimated (15 of 17 items) |
| WSJF Prioritization | Not Implemented | Effort and Business Value fields exist but are empty on all 36 Features |
| PI Objectives | Implemented | 6 PI Objectives defined — good practice |
| ROAM Risk Management | Not Implemented | 0 risks/impediments logged |
| Backlog Refinement | Partial | Items in "Grooming" state suggest some refinement occurs, but stale items from 8 months ago indicate it is not systematic |
| Inspect & Adapt | Unknown | No IP iteration in PI 6; no I&A artifacts visible |

---

## 13. Recommendations

### 13.1 Immediate Actions (This Week — Days 2–5 of Sprint)

| # | Action | Owner | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Configure team capacity** for armelita, Samantha Babael, and Teofilo Limpag with actual hours/day | Scrum Master / Team Lead | Critical |
| 2 | **Add Samantha Babael and Teofilo Limpag** to the JIT Operation Team roster in Azure DevOps | Project Admin | Critical |
| 3 | **Clarify TESDA AC compliance deadline** — if there is a hard regulatory date, the 7 compliance items (IDs: 199496–199503) must be prioritized above all other work | armelita | High |
| 4 | **Resolve item 198637** (Markdown Training Dry-run) — complete grooming and move to "Ready" or defer to next sprint | Samantha Babael | Medium |
| 5 | **Estimate 2 unestimated items** (198630 Markdown Training, 199221 ChatGPT Courseware) | Team | Medium |
| 6 | **Move "New" items to "Active"** as work begins — update board states daily to reflect actual progress | All team members | Medium |

### 13.2 Before Sprint End (March 8, 2026)

| # | Action | Owner | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | **Record sprint actuals** — document how many of the 23 story points were completed; this becomes the team's first velocity baseline | Scrum Master | High |
| 8 | **Update PI Objective states** — reflect true progress before PI 6 closes | armelita | High |
| 9 | **Conduct Iteration Review** — demo completed work to stakeholders, especially TESDA compliance deliverables | Team | Medium |
| 10 | **Conduct Iteration Retrospective** — discuss: Why is capacity not configured? Is the workload distribution sustainable? Are items entering sprints in "Ready" state? | Team | Medium |

### 13.3 For PI 7 Planning

| # | Action | Owner | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| 11 | **Redistribute workload** — target no single team member owning more than 40% of sprint items | Scrum Master | High |
| 12 | **Conduct backlog grooming** — review and remove/re-plan 20 stale items from PI 3 and root iteration | Team | High |
| 13 | **Include an IP iteration** in PI 7 — protect time for innovation, hackathons, and Inspect & Adapt | RTE / Program Manager | High |
| 14 | **Populate Effort and Business Value** on all Features to enable WSJF prioritization | Product Owner | Medium |
| 15 | **Start logging risks** in the Risks and Impediments backlog — use ROAM categorization | Team | Medium |
| 16 | **Establish velocity baseline** — use Iteration 6.4 actuals + first 2 sprints of PI 7 to calculate average velocity for future planning | Scrum Master | Medium |

### 13.4 Recommendation Priority Matrix

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Recommendation Priority — Effort vs Impact
    x-axis Low Effort --> High Effort
    y-axis Low Impact --> High Impact
    quadrant-1 Plan & Schedule
    quadrant-2 Do First
    quadrant-3 Defer
    quadrant-4 Quick Wins
    "Configure Capacity": [0.15, 0.90]

    "Backlog Grooming (Stale)": [0.55, 0.65]
    "Populate WSJF Fields": [0.70, 0.60]
    "Add IP Iteration to PI 7": [0.40, 0.70]
    "Start ROAM Risk Tracking": [0.30, 0.55]
```

---

## 14. Conclusion

The JIT Operation Team demonstrates **promising SAFe fundamentals** — properly typed PI Objectives, story-driven delivery (82% User Stories), reasonable story point adoption (88%), and right-sized work items (1–3 points). These are signs of a team that understands the mechanics of agile delivery.

However, **critical process gaps** prevent the team from realizing the benefits of these fundamentals:

1. **Zero configured capacity** makes planning and tracking meaningless
2. **76% workload concentration on one person** creates unacceptable organizational risk
3. **No risk management** leaves the team blind to impediments
4. **Stale backlog items** (8+ months) indicate insufficient refinement discipline
5. **No IP iteration** removes protected time for improvement

The path forward is clear: **fix capacity and workload balance first** (Quick Wins), then **establish velocity and refinement cadence** (Short Term), and finally **implement WSJF and formalize I&A** (Medium Term). The team has the foundation — it now needs the discipline.

---

*Audit report generated by Claude AI Agile PM Consultant on 2026-02-24. Data sourced live from Azure DevOps (`dev.azure.com/jairo`, Jairosoft Portfolio project, JIT Operation Team board, Iteration 6.4). Based on SAFe 6.0 framework standards from [ScaledAgileFramework.com](https://ScaledAgileFramework.com).*

   112→**Finding:** In the **final iteration of PI 6**, 3 of 6 PI Objectives (50%) remain in "New" state. Per SAFe, PI Objectives should be actively worked toward throughout the PI. Objectives still in "New" in the last iteration suggest either:
   113→- The objectives were not properly decomposed into Features/Stories
   114→- Work started but the objective status was never updated
   115→- The objectives are at risk of not being met this PI
   116→
   117→**Recommendation:** Before PI 6 closes, update all PI Objective states to reflect reality. During the upcoming Inspect & Adapt, formally assess which objectives were met vs. missed.

   259→### 6.3 Finding — Critical Workload Imbalance

   261→**armelita carries 76% of all sprint work (13 items, 18 of 23 story points).** This is the single most critical finding in this audit.
   262→

   268→| **Quality Risk** | High volume + single person = reduced time for thorough work |
   269→
   270→**SAFe Guidance:** SAFe recommends work be distributed across team members to promote collective ownership and reduce dependency risk. No single team member should own more than 30–40% of a sprint's committed work.

   562→| 13 | **Include an IP iteration** in PI 7 — protect time for innovation, hackathons, and Inspect & Adapt | RTE / Program Manager | High |
   563→| 14 | **Populate Effort and Business Value** on all Features to enable WSJF prioritization | Product Owner | Medium |
   564→| 15 | **Start logging risks** in the Risks and Impediments backlog — use ROAM categorization | Team | Medium |
   565→| 16 | **Establish velocity baseline** — use Iteration 6.4 actuals + first 2 sprints of PI 7 to calculate average velocity for future planning | Scrum Master | Medium |
   566→
   567→### 13.4 Recommendation Priority Matrix
   568→
   569→```mermaid
   570→quadrantChart
   571→    title Recommendation Priority — Effort vs Impact
   572→    x-axis Low Effort --> High Effort
   573→    y-axis Low Impact --> High Impact
   574→    quadrant-1 Plan & Schedule
   575→    quadrant-2 Do First
   576→    quadrant-3 Defer
   577→    quadrant-4 Quick Wins
   578→    "Configure Capacity": [0.15, 0.90]
   579→    "Add Team Members to Roster": [0.10, 0.70]
   580→    "Clarify TESDA Deadline": [0.20, 0.85]
   581→    "Update Board States Daily": [0.10, 0.60]
   582→    "Record Velocity Baseline": [0.25, 0.75]
   583→    "Redistribute Workload": [0.65, 0.90]
   584→    "Backlog Grooming (Stale)": [0.55, 0.65]
   585→    "Populate WSJF Fields": [0.70, 0.60]
   586→    "Add IP Iteration to PI 7": [0.40, 0.70]
   587→    "Start ROAM Risk Tracking": [0.30, 0.55]
   588→```
   589→
   590→---
   591→
   592→## 14. Conclusion
   593→
   594→The JIT Operation Team demonstrates **promising SAFe fundamentals** — properly typed PI Objectives, story-driven delivery (82% User Stories), reasonable story point adoption (88%), and right-sized work items (1–3 points). These are signs of a team that understands the mechanics of agile delivery.
   595→
   596→However, **critical process gaps** prevent the team from realizing the benefits of these fundamentals:
   597→
   598→1. **Zero configured capacity** makes planning and tracking meaningless
   599→2. **76% workload concentration on one person** creates unacceptable organizational risk
   600→3. **No risk management** leaves the team blind to impediments
   601→4. **Stale backlog items** (8+ months) indicate insufficient refinement discipline
   602→5. **No IP iteration** removes protected time for improvement
   603→
   604→The path forward is clear: **fix capacity and workload balance first** (Quick Wins), then **establish velocity and refinement cadence** (Short Term), and finally **implement WSJF and formalize I&A** (Medium Term). The team has the foundation — it now needs the discipline.
   605→
   606→---
   607→
   608→*Audit report generated by Claude AI Agile PM Consultant on 2026-02-24. Data sourced live from Azure DevOps (`dev.azure.com/jairo`, Jairosoft Portfolio project, JIT Operation Team board, Iteration 6.4). Based on SAFe 6.0 framework standards from [ScaledAgileFramework.com](https://ScaledAgileFramework.com).*
   609→

# SAFe Iteration Audit Report — JIT Operation Team, Iteration 6.4

**Audit Date:** 2026-02-24
**Auditor:** Claude AI (Agile PM Consultant, 10+ years SAFe experience)
**Framework:** Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) 6.0
**Organization:** Jairosoft LLC (`dev.azure.com/jairo`)
**Project:** Jairosoft Portfolio
**Team:** JIT Operation Team
**Scope:** Iteration 6.4 (February 23 – March 8, 2026)
**PI Context:** 2026-PI6, Final Iteration (4 of 4)

---

## 1. Executive Summary

This audit examines the **JIT Operation Team's** board and current sprint (Iteration 6.4) within the Jairosoft Portfolio project. The JIT Operation Team is classified as a **Shared Services** team responsible for training operations, TESDA certification/compliance, courseware development, intern programs, and partnerships for the Jairo Institute of Technology.

The team is in **Day 2 of a 14-day sprint** with 17 work items committed totaling 23 story points. While the sprint demonstrates strong fundamentals — story-driven delivery, proper estimation, and right-sized work items — critical process gaps in capacity planning, workload balance, and risk management severely undermine execution predictability.

### Overall Iteration Health

| Dimension | Score | Rating |
|---|---|---|
| Capacity Planning | 1/10 | Critical |
| Workload Balance | 2/10 | Critical |
| Risk Management | 1/10 | Critical |
| State Discipline | 4/10 | Poor |
| Sprint Scope | 5/10 | Moderate |
| Estimation Quality | 7/10 | Good |
| Work Item Types | 8/10 | Strong |
| Story Point Adoption | 8/10 | Strong |

**Overall Iteration Score: 4.5 / 10 — Needs Significant Improvement**

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Iteration 6.4 Sprint Health — JIT Operation Team
    x-axis Low Health --> High Health
    y-axis Low Risk --> High Risk
    quadrant-1 Fix Immediately
    quadrant-2 Monitor Closely
    quadrant-3 Acceptable
    quadrant-4 Improve Next Sprint
    "Capacity Planning": [0.05, 0.90]
    "Workload Balance": [0.15, 0.85]
    "Story Point Adoption": [0.80, 0.20]
    "Work Item Types": [0.75, 0.25]
    "State Discipline": [0.30, 0.70]
    "Risk Management": [0.10, 0.65]
    "Sprint Scope": [0.45, 0.55]
    "Estimation Quality": [0.70, 0.30]
```

---

## 2. Team Profile

### 2.1 Team Roster (Azure DevOps)

The JIT Operation Team has **2 registered members** in Azure DevOps:

| Member | Email | Registered | Capacity (Iteration 6.4) |
|---|---|---|---|
| armelita | armelita@jairosoft.com | Yes | **0 hrs/day** |
| grace | grace@jairosoft.com | Yes | **0 hrs/day** |

**Additional contributors** (not registered as team members):

| Contributor | Items in Sprint | Registered |
|---|---|---|
| Samantha Babael | 3 | No |
| Teofilo Limpag | 1 | No |

**Total team capacity configured: 0 hrs/day**

### 2.2 Board Configuration

The team's board is configured with **6 backlog levels**:

| Backlog Level | Work Item Types | Visible | Items |
|---|---|---|---|
| Goals and Releases | Goal, Release | Hidden | -- |
| Epics and Objectives | Epic, PI Objective | Visible | 6 |
| Features and Enablers | Feature, Enabler Feature, Change Request | Visible | 36 |
| Stories and Deliverables | User Story, Enabler, Spike, Courseware, Training, Design, Defect, Tech Debt | Visible | 42 |
| Risks and Impediments | Issue, Risk | Visible | **0** |
| Tasks | Task, Peer Review, Bug | Visible | -- |

**Total board work items: 84**

---

## 3. PI 6 Context — PI Objectives

The JIT Operation Team has **6 PI Objectives** defined for 2026-PI6. All are typed as "PI Objective" at the Epic level — a correct SAFe practice.

| ID | PI Objective | State | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|
| 197441 | Produce Professional Alumni | Active | armelita |
| 197443 | Recognized as CSS NC II Assessment Center | Active | armelita |
| 197445 | Launch new courses | Active | armelita |
| 197444 | CSS NC II Assessment Offering | New | Unassigned |
| 197446 | Use OpenEduCat for JIT Operations | New | armelita |
| 197450 | Onboard Qualified Interns for eLMS and jit.edu.ph | New | armelita |

```mermaid
pie title PI 6 Objective States
    "Active" : 3
    "New" : 3
```

**Finding:** In the **final iteration of PI 6**, 3 of 6 PI Objectives (50%) remain in "New" state. Per SAFe, PI Objectives should be actively worked toward throughout the PI. Objectives still in "New" in the last iteration suggest either:
- The objectives were not properly decomposed into Features/Stories
- Work started but the objective status was never updated
- The objectives are at risk of not being met this PI

**Recommendation:** Before PI 6 closes, update all PI Objective states to reflect reality. During the upcoming Inspect & Adapt, formally assess which objectives were met vs. missed.

---

## 4. Iteration Structure — PI 6 Timeline

The JIT Operation Team is subscribed to iterations spanning PI 3 through PI 6:

### PI 6 Iteration Schedule

```mermaid
gantt
    title 2026-PI6 — JIT Operation Team Iterations
    dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD
    section PI 6
    Iteration 6.1 (Completed)   :done, 2026-01-12, 2026-01-25
    Iteration 6.2 (Completed)   :done, 2026-01-26, 2026-02-08
    Iteration 6.3 (Completed)   :done, 2026-02-09, 2026-02-22
    Iteration 6.4 (CURRENT)     :active, 2026-02-23, 2026-03-08
```

| Iteration | Start | End | Duration | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 6.1 | Jan 12, 2026 | Jan 25, 2026 | 2 weeks | Completed |
| 6.2 | Jan 26, 2026 | Feb 08, 2026 | 2 weeks | Completed |
| 6.3 | Feb 09, 2026 | Feb 22, 2026 | 2 weeks | Completed |
| **6.4** | **Feb 23, 2026** | **Mar 08, 2026** | **2 weeks** | **Current** |

**Finding:** PI 6 has **4 iterations** (8 weeks) instead of the standard SAFe 5+1 IP pattern (12 weeks). Earlier PIs (PI 3, PI 4) had 5–6 iterations each. This shortened PI may indicate:
- A deliberate cadence change
- Incomplete iteration configuration
- Missing Innovation & Planning (IP) iteration

**SAFe Note:** The absence of a dedicated IP iteration means no protected time for innovation, planning, and Inspect & Adapt. This should be addressed for PI 7.

---

## 5. Iteration 6.4 — Sprint Backlog Audit

### 5.1 Sprint Metrics Summary

| Metric | Value | SAFe Benchmark | Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Work Items | 17 | 8–12 per team | **Over-committed** |
| Total Story Points | 23 | Based on velocity | Unknown (no baseline) |
| Items Estimated | 15 of 17 (88%) | 100% | Good but incomplete |
| Unestimated Items | 2 | 0 | Gap |
| Team Capacity | 0 hrs/day | Must be >0 | **Not configured** |
| Risks Logged | 0 | Track actively | **Critical gap** |
| Items in "New" (Day 2) | 10 of 17 (59%) | <20% by Day 2 | **Poor flow** |
| Items "Active" | 1 of 17 (6%) | 20–40% | **Stalled** |

### 5.2 Full Sprint Backlog

| # | ID | Type | Title | State | Assigned To | Pts |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 199246 | User Story | Duplicate eLMS COC 1 | **Active** | Teofilo Limpag | 2 |
| 2 | 197617 | User Story | Signing of Agreement on SK Buhangin Partnership | Ready for Dev | armelita | 1 |
| 3 | 198612 | User Story | Follow up Sam Application as Trainer | Ready for Dev | armelita | 1 |
| 4 | 198630 | Training | Markdown Training for the Employees | Ready | Samantha Babael | -- |
| 5 | 199221 | Courseware | ChatGPT Courseware | Ready | Samantha Babael | -- |
| 6 | 198637 | User Story | Markdown Training Dry-run | **Grooming** | Samantha Babael | 1 |
| 7 | 198615 | User Story | Awarding of CSS NC II Certificates | New | armelita | 2 |
| 8 | 199092 | User Story | Submit TESDA Career Guidance Semestral Report CY 2026 | New | armelita | 2 |
| 9 | 199489 | User Story | Interview and Onboard Cor Jesu Interns | New | armelita | 2 |
| 10 | 199496 | User Story | CSS NC II CTC SO Certificate | New | armelita | 1 |
| 11 | 199498 | User Story | Get Copy of Lacking Admin Docs from Ma'am Grace | New | armelita | 1 |
| 12 | 199499 | User Story | Update Company Profile for AC Compliance | New | armelita | 1 |
| 13 | 199500 | User Story | Get Notarized Contract of Employees for AC Compliance | New | armelita | 1 |
| 14 | 199501 | User Story | Get Copy of Building Layout, Shop Layout, and Floor Plan | New | armelita | 1 |
| 15 | 199502 | User Story | Accomplish Checklist F04 AC Compliance | New | armelita | 1 |
| 16 | 199503 | User Story | Repackage AC Compliance | New | armelita | 2 |
| 17 | 199505 | User Story | Contact Inquirers for their downpayment | New | armelita | 3 |

### 5.3 Work Item State Distribution

```mermaid
pie title Iteration 6.4 — Work Item States (Day 2)
    "New" : 10
    "Ready for Dev" : 2
    "Ready" : 2
    "Grooming" : 1
    "Active" : 1
```

**Analysis:**
- **59% New** — The majority of sprint work has not entered the team's workflow. On Day 2 of a 14-day sprint, this signals either late sprint planning or work items that were added without proper commitment.
- **12% Ready for Dev** — 2 items are refined and ready to be picked up.
- **12% Ready** — 2 items (Training + Courseware) are prepped but haven't been pulled.
- **6% Grooming** — 1 item is still being refined inside the sprint. **This is a SAFe violation** — items should meet Definition of Ready before entering an iteration.
- **6% Active** — Only 1 item has actual work in progress.

### 5.4 Work Item Type Distribution

```mermaid
pie title Iteration 6.4 — Work Item Types
    "User Story" : 14
    "Training" : 1
    "Courseware" : 1
    "Spike" : 1
```

| Type | Count | Points | % of Sprint |
|---|---|---|---|
| User Story | 14 | 21 | 82% |
| Training | 1 | -- | 6% |
| Courseware | 1 | -- | 6% |
| Spike | 1 | -- | 6% |
| **Total** | **17** | **23** | **100%** |

**Positive Finding:** User Stories account for **82% of the sprint**. This is a major improvement over the portfolio-wide observation where Spikes dominated at 42% and User Stories were only 6%. The JIT Operation Team is properly using User Stories as the primary vehicle for value delivery — a SAFe best practice.

---

## 6. Workload Distribution Analysis

### 6.1 Load by Team Member

```mermaid
pie title Sprint Load by Team Member (Items)
    "armelita (13 items)" : 13
    "Samantha Babael (3 items)" : 3
    "Teofilo Limpag (1 item)" : 1
```

| Team Member | Items | Story Points | % Load | Item States |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **armelita** | **13** | **18 pts** | **76%** | 10 New, 2 Ready for Dev, 1 New |
| Samantha Babael | 3 | 2 pts | 18% | 1 Ready, 1 Grooming, 1 Ready |
| Teofilo Limpag | 1 | 2 pts | 6% | 1 Active |
| **Total** | **17** | **23 pts** | **100%** | |

### 6.2 Workload Imbalance Visualization

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Story Points by Team Member"
    x-axis ["armelita", "Samantha Babael", "Teofilo Limpag"]
    y-axis "Story Points" 0 --> 20
    bar [18, 2, 2]
```

### 6.3 Finding — Critical Workload Imbalance

**armelita carries 76% of all sprint work (13 items, 18 of 23 story points).** This is the single most critical finding in this audit.

| Risk Factor | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Bus Factor** | If armelita is unavailable for even 2–3 days, 76% of the sprint is at risk |
| **Bottleneck** | All 10 "New" items are hers — nothing moves until she starts them |
| **Burnout Risk** | 18 story points in 10 working days = ~1.8 pts/day sustained |
| **Quality Risk** | High volume + single person = reduced time for thorough work |

**SAFe Guidance:** SAFe recommends work be distributed across team members to promote collective ownership and reduce dependency risk. No single team member should own more than 30–40% of a sprint's committed work.

---

## 7. Sprint Theme Analysis

The 17 work items cluster into **4 strategic themes**:

```mermaid
graph TD

        A3["199501 · Building Layouts & Floor Plan"]
        A4["199502 · Checklist F04"]
        A5["199503 · Repackage AC Compliance"]
        A6["199496 · CSS NC II CTC SO Certificate"]
        A7["199498 · Get Admin Docs from Grace"]
        T1H --- A1 & A2 & A3 & A4 & A5 & A6 & A7
    end
    subgraph "Theme 2: Training & Courseware"
        direction TB
        T2H["3 items | 1 pt | Samantha Babael"]
        B1["198630 · Markdown Training"]
        B2["198637 · Markdown Dry-run"]
        B3["199221 · ChatGPT Courseware"]
        T2H --- B1 & B2 & B3
    end
    subgraph "Theme 3: Partnerships & Onboarding"
        direction TB
        T3H["4 items | 7 pts | armelita"]

        C2["199489 · Cor Jesu Interns"]
        C3["198612 · Sam Trainer Application"]
        C4["199505 · Contact Inquirers for Downpayment"]
        T3H --- C1 & C2 & C3 & C4
    end
    subgraph "Theme 4: Certification & Reporting"
        direction TB
        T4H["3 items | 6 pts | Mixed"]
        D1["198615 · CSS NC II Certificates"]
        D2["199092 · TESDA Semestral Report"]
        D3["199246 · Duplicate eLMS COC 1"]
        T4H --- D1 & D2 & D3
    end
    style T1H fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff
    style T2H fill:#3498db,color:#fff
    style T3H fill:#2ecc71,color:#fff
    style T4H fill:#9b59b6,color:#fff
```

| Theme | Items | Points | Owner(s) | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TESDA AC Compliance | 7 (41%) | 9 | armelita (100%) | **High** — regulatory, all "New" |
| Training & Courseware | 3 (18%) | 1 | Samantha Babael | Low |
| Partnerships & Onboarding | 4 (24%) | 7 | armelita (100%) | Medium |
| Certification & Reporting | 3 (18%) | 6 | Mixed | Medium |

**Key Insight:** The sprint is dominated by **TESDA Assessment Center compliance** (41% of items). These 7 items form a dependency cluster — they all relate to preparing documentation for regulatory compliance. If there is a hard TESDA deadline, these should be prioritized above all other work. Currently, all 7 are in "New" state on Day 2.

---

## 8. Capacity Analysis

### 8.1 Configured Capacity

| Member | Capacity/Day | Days Off | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| armelita | **0 hrs** | 0 | Registered member |
| grace | **0 hrs** | 0 | Registered member (no sprint items) |
| Samantha Babael | **Not in roster** | -- | Contributing but unregistered |
| Teofilo Limpag | **Not in roster** | -- | Contributing but unregistered |
| **Team Total** | **0 hrs/day** | **0** | |

### 8.2 Cross-Team Comparison (Iteration 6.4)

| Team | Capacity/Day | Days Off |
|---|---|---|
| Colina Health Product Team | 14.5 hrs | 2 |
| JIT Site eLMS Product Team | 48 hrs | 0 |
| **JIT Operation Team** | **0 hrs** | **0** |

**Finding:** The JIT Operation Team is the **only team with zero configured capacity** among the teams active in Iteration 6.4. Other teams (Colina Health at 14.5 hrs/day, JIT eLMS at 48 hrs/day) have properly configured capacity, demonstrating that the organization knows how to set this up — the JIT Operation Team simply hasn't done it.

### 8.3 Impact of Zero Capacity

Without configured capacity:
1. **Burndown charts** show no baseline — the team cannot track progress visually
2. **Sprint commitment validation** is impossible — there is no way to know if 23 points is achievable
3. **Azure DevOps capacity alerts** will not trigger — the system cannot warn about over-allocation
4. **Velocity tracking** has no anchor — historical capacity-to-delivery ratios cannot be calculated

---

## 9. Backlog Health — Full Board Analysis

Beyond Iteration 6.4, the full JIT Operation Team board reveals systemic backlog health issues.

### 9.1 Board Inventory

| Backlog Level | Total Items | Active | New | Other |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Epics (PI Objectives) | 6 | 3 | 3 | 0 |
| Features | 36 | 15 | 17 | 4 (3 Req. Gathering, 1 Enabler) |
| Stories & Deliverables | 42 | 2 | 15 | 25 |
| Risks & Impediments | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **Total** | **84** | **20** | **35** | **29** |

### 9.2 Stale Work Items

```mermaid
pie title Stale Items by Origin PI
    "PI 3 (Jun–Sep 2025)" : 10
```

| 188995 | Courseware | Introduction to Rust Language Programming | New | 8 months |

**SAFe Guidance:** Backlog items that have not been touched for 2+ PIs should be formally reviewed during backlog refinement. Items that are no longer relevant should be **Removed** (not left as "New") to maintain backlog transparency and reduce cognitive overhead.

### 9.3 Feature Effort & Business Value

| Feature Field | Configured | Populated |

**Finding:** The board schema includes **Effort** and **Business Value** fields at the Feature level (required for WSJF calculation), but **none of the 36 Features have values populated**. This means **Weighted Shortest Job First (WSJF) prioritization is impossible** — a core SAFe Lean Portfolio Management practice.

---

## 10. State Flow & Process Compliance

### 10.1 Workflow States Observed

The team uses the following workflow states:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> New
    New --> RequirementsGathering : Refine
    RequirementsGathering --> Grooming : Groom
    Grooming --> Ready : Ready
    Ready --> ReadyForDev : Commit
    ReadyForDev --> Active : Start
    Active --> Validation : Complete
    Validation --> Closed : Accept

    state "New" as New
    state "Requirements Gathering" as RequirementsGathering
    state "Grooming" as Grooming
    state "Ready" as Ready
    state "Ready for Dev" as ReadyForDev
    state "Active" as Active
    state "Validation" as Validation
    state "Closed" as Closed
```

### 10.2 SAFe Process Violations

| Violation | Item(s) | SAFe Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Item in "Grooming" inside active sprint | 198637 (Markdown Training Dry-run) | Items must meet Definition of Ready before entering an iteration |
| PI Objectives still "New" in final iteration | 197444, 197446, 197450 | PI Objectives should be actively tracked throughout the PI |
| Zero risks logged | Entire board | Teams should track risks using ROAM (Resolved, Owned, Accepted, Mitigated) |
| No capacity configured | All team members | Capacity must be set for burndown and commitment validation |
| Stale items from prior PIs | 10 items from PI 3 | Backlog refinement should clean up stale items each PI |

---

## 11. Risk Register

| # | Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Category | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **R1** | **Single point of failure** — armelita owns 76% of sprint work | High | Critical | People | Redistribute work; cross-train Samantha and Teofilo; add team members |
| **R2** | **Zero capacity configured** — sprint commitment is unvalidated | Confirmed | High | Process | Configure capacity for armelita, Samantha, and Teofilo immediately |
| **R3** | **TESDA AC Compliance deadline risk** — 7 regulatory items all "New" on Day 2 | Medium | High | Regulatory | Clarify TESDA deadline; if time-bound, prioritize above all other sprint work |
| **R4** | **PI Objectives at risk** — 50% still "New" in final iteration | High | Medium | Delivery | Assess PI Objective achievability; document unmet objectives for I&A |
| **R5** | **Unregistered team members** — Samantha and Teofilo not in ADO roster | Confirmed | Medium | Governance | Add them to the JIT Operation Team in ADO project settings |
| **R6** | **Backlog debt** — 20 stale items (PI 3 + root) creating noise | Confirmed | Low | Process | Conduct backlog refinement session; remove or re-plan stale items |
| **R7** | **No velocity baseline** — team cannot forecast future sprints | Confirmed | Medium | Planning | Track Iteration 6.4 actuals to establish first velocity data point |
| **R8** | **No IP iteration in PI 6** — no protected time for innovation or I&A | Confirmed | Medium | SAFe Compliance | Plan IP iteration for PI 7; schedule I&A ceremony at PI 6 close |

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Risk Assessment — JIT Operation Team
    x-axis Low Likelihood --> High Likelihood
    y-axis Low Impact --> High Impact
    quadrant-1 Mitigate Immediately
    quadrant-2 Monitor & Plan
    quadrant-3 Accept
    quadrant-4 Address Proactively
    "R1 Single Point of Failure": [0.85, 0.95]
    "R2 Zero Capacity": [0.95, 0.80]
    "R3 TESDA Deadline": [0.55, 0.85]
    "R4 PI Objectives at Risk": [0.75, 0.65]
    "R5 Unregistered Members": [0.95, 0.45]
    "R6 Backlog Debt": [0.95, 0.30]
    "R7 No Velocity Baseline": [0.95, 0.50]
    "R8 No IP Iteration": [0.95, 0.50]
```

---

## 12. SAFe Compliance Assessment

### 12.1 SAFe Core Values

| Core Value | Status | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| **Alignment** | Partial | PI Objectives exist and are typed correctly. However, 50% are "New" in the final iteration, suggesting weak traceability from objectives to sprint work. |
| **Built-in Quality** | Unknown | No evidence of Definition of Done, acceptance criteria, or peer review tasks in the sprint backlog. |
| **Transparency** | Poor | Capacity = 0 makes burndown invisible. Risks/Impediments backlog is empty. Stale items obscure true backlog health. |
| **Program Execution** | Partial | Iteration cadence is maintained (2-week sprints). But work isn't flowing — 59% still "New" on Day 2. |

### 12.2 SAFe Essential Practices

| Practice | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Iteration Planning | Partial | Items assigned to sprint, but capacity not set and many items are "New" — suggests items were added without a formal planning ceremony |
| Definition of Ready | Not Enforced | 1 item in "Grooming" state is in the sprint |

The JIT Operation Team demonstrates **promising SAFe fundamentals** — properly typed PI Objectives, story-driven delivery (82% User Stories), reasonable story point adoption (88%), and right-sized work items (1–3 points). These are signs of a team that understands the mechanics of agile delivery.

However, **critical process gaps** prevent the team from realizing the benefits of these fundamentals:

1. **Zero configured capacity** makes planning and tracking meaningless
2. **76% workload concentration on one person** creates unacceptable organizational risk
3. **No risk management** leaves the team blind to impediments
4. **Stale backlog items** (8+ months) indicate insufficient refinement discipline
5. **No IP iteration** removes protected time for improvement

The path forward is clear: **fix capacity and workload balance first** (Quick Wins), then **establish velocity and refinement cadence** (Short Term), and finally **implement WSJF and formalize I&A** (Medium Term). The team has the foundation — it now needs the discipline.

title Recommendation Priority — Effort vs Impact

-The JIT Operation Team demonstrates **promising SAFe fundamentals** — properly typed PI Objectives, story-driven delivery (82% User Stories), reasonable story point adoption (88%), and right-sized work items (1–3 points). These are signs of a team that understands the mechanics of agile delivery.
+Iteration 6.4 reveals a team with **good agile instincts but critical process gaps**:
-However, **critical process gaps** prevent the team from realizing the benefits of these fundamentals:
+- **82% User Stories** — story-driven delivery is strong
+- **88% estimated** — the team values sizing their work
+- **Right-sized items (1–3 pts)** — work is properly decomposed
+- **Clear thematic focus** — TESDA compliance, training, partnerships, and certification
-1. **Zero configured capacity** makes planning and tracking meaningless
-2. **76% workload concentration on one person** creates unacceptable organizational risk
-3. **No risk management** leaves the team blind to impediments
-4. **Stale backlog items** (8+ months) indicate insufficient refinement discipline
-5. **No IP iteration** removes protected time for improvement
+- **Zero capacity configured** — burndown and commitment validation are impossible
+- **76% workload on one person** — armelita is a single point of failure carrying 18 of 23 story points
+- **59% of items still "New" on Day 2** — work is not flowing through the board
+- **Zero risks logged** — the team is blind to impediments
+- **2 active contributors not in the team roster** — their work is invisible to ADO tracking

-The path forward is clear: **fix capacity and workload balance first** (Quick Wins), then **establish velocity and refinement cadence** (Short Term), and finally **implement WSJF and formalize I&A** (Medium Term). The team has the foundation — it now needs the discipline.

+The fix is straightforward: **configure capacity, register team members, and start moving items through the board daily.** These are low-effort, high-impact actions that can be completed within this sprint.

# SAFe Iteration Audit Report — JIT Operation Team, Iteration 6.4

**Audit Date:** 2026-02-24
**Auditor:** Claude AI (Agile PM Consultant, 10+ years SAFe experience)
**Framework:** Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) 6.0
**Organization:** Jairosoft LLC (`dev.azure.com/jairo`)
**Project:** Jairosoft Portfolio
**Team:** JIT Operation Team
**Scope:** Iteration 6.4 (February 23 – March 8, 2026)
**PI Context:** 2026-PI6, Final Iteration (4 of 4)

---

## 1. Executive Summary

This audit examines the **JIT Operation Team's** board and current sprint (Iteration 6.4) within the Jairosoft Portfolio project. The JIT Operation Team is classified as a **Shared Services** team responsible for training operations, TESDA certification/compliance, courseware development, intern programs, and partnerships for the Jairo Institute of Technology.

The team is in **Day 2 of a 14-day sprint** with 17 work items committed totaling 23 story points. While the sprint demonstrates strong fundamentals — story-driven delivery, proper estimation, and right-sized work items — critical process gaps in capacity planning, workload balance, and risk management severely undermine execution predictability.

### Overall Iteration Health

|---|---|---|
| Capacity Planning | 1/10 | Critical |
| Workload Balance | 2/10 | Critical |

| Estimation Quality | 7/10 | Good |

| Story Point Adoption | 8/10 | Strong |

**Overall Iteration Score: 4.5 / 10 — Needs Significant Improvement**

## 3. PI 6 Context — PI Objectives

The JIT Operation Team has **6 PI Objectives** defined for 2026-PI6. All are typed as "PI Objective" at the Epic level — a correct SAFe practice.

**Finding:** In the **final iteration of PI 6**, 3 of 6 PI Objectives (50%) remain in "New" state. Per SAFe, PI Objectives should be actively worked toward throughout the PI. Objectives still in "New" in the last iteration suggest either:
- The objectives were not properly decomposed into Features/Stories
- Work started but the objective status was never updated
- The objectives are at risk of not being met this PI

**Recommendation:** Before PI 6 closes, update all PI Objective states to reflect reality. During the upcoming Inspect & Adapt, formally assess which objectives were met vs. missed.

- A deliberate cadence change

- Missing Innovation & Planning (IP) iteration

### 6.3 Finding — Critical Workload Imbalance

**armelita carries 76% of all sprint work (13 items, 18 of 23 story points).** This is the single most critical finding in this audit.

| Risk Factor | Detail |
|---|---|

| **Burnout Risk** | 18 story points in 10 working days = ~1.8 pts/day sustained |
| **Quality Risk** | High volume + single person = reduced time for thorough work |

**SAFe Guidance:** SAFe recommends work be distributed across team members to promote collective ownership and reduce dependency risk. No single team member should own more than 30–40% of a sprint's committed work.

**SAFe Guidance:** Backlog items that have not been touched for 2+ PIs should be formally reviewed during backlog refinement. Items that are no longer relevant should be **Removed** (not left as "New") to maintain backlog transparency and reduce cognitive overhead.

## 14. Conclusion

The JIT Operation Team demonstrates **promising SAFe fundamentals** — properly typed PI Objectives, story-driven delivery (82% User Stories), reasonable story point adoption (88%), and right-sized work items (1–3 points). These are signs of a team that understands the mechanics of agile delivery.

However, **critical process gaps** prevent the team from realizing the benefits of these fundamentals:

1. **Zero configured capacity** makes planning and tracking meaningless
2. **76% workload concentration on one person** creates unacceptable organizational risk
3. **No risk management** leaves the team blind to impediments
4. **Stale backlog items** (8+ months) indicate insufficient refinement discipline
5. **No IP iteration** removes protected time for improvement

The path forward is clear: **fix capacity and workload balance first** (Quick Wins), then **establish velocity and refinement cadence** (Short Term), and finally **implement WSJF and formalize I&A** (Medium Term). The team has the foundation — it now needs the discipline.

---
